```
1. import { defineCollection, reference } from 'astro:content';
2. import { glob, file } from 'astro/loaders';
3. import { z } from 'astro/zod';
4. 
5. // ========================
6. // PRESENTATIONS COLLECTION
7. // ========================
8. const presentations = defineCollection({
9.     loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.md", base: "./src/content/presentations" }),
10.     schema: z.object({
11.         title: z.string(),
12.         slug: z.string().optional(),
13.         description: z.string(),
14.         type: z.enum([
15.             'session',
16.             'workshop',
17.             'lightning-talk',
18.             'keynote',
19.             'panel',
20.             'webinar'
21.         ]).default('session'),
22.         // Support multiple presentation lengths (e.g., 45, 60, 75 minute versions)
23.         durations: z.array(z.number()).optional(),
24.         level: z.enum(['introductory', 'intermediate', 'advanced', 'all']).default('all'),
25. 
26.         // Learning objectives
27.         learningObjectives: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
28. 
29.         // Categorization
30.         tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
31. 
32.         // Related presentations
33.         relatedPresentations: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
34. 
35.         // Resources
36.         resources: z.array(z.object({
37.             type: z.enum(['slides', 'video', 'github', 'code', 'demo', 'blog', 'download', 'documentation', 'other']),
38.             title: z.string(),
39.             url: z.string(),
40.             description: z.string().optional(),
41.         })).optional(),
42. 
43.         // Media
44.         heroImage: z.string().optional(),
45. 
46.         // Status
47.         status: z.enum(['active', 'retired', 'in-development']).default('active'),
48.         featured: z.boolean().default(false),
49.     }),
50. });
51. 
52. // ========================
53. // SPEAKING EVENTS COLLECTION
54. // ========================
55. const events = defineCollection({
56.     loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.md", base: "./src/content/events" }),
57.     schema: z.object({
58.         title: z.string(),
59.         slug: z.string().optional(),
60.         eventType: z.enum([
61.             'conference',
62.             'meetup',
63.             'webinar',
64.             'podcast',
65.             'workshop',
66.             'user-group',
67.             'corporate',
68.             'other'
69.         ]).default('conference'),
70.         description: z.string().optional(),
71. 
72.         // Event details
73.         startDate: z.coerce.date(),
74.         endDate: z.coerce.date().optional(),
75.         location: z.object({
76.             venue: z.string().optional(),
77.             city: z.string(),
78.             state: z.string().optional(),
79.             country: z.string(),
80.         }),
81. 
82.         // Linked presentation(s)
83.         presentations: z.array(z.union([
84.             z.string(), // Simple presentation ID for backward compatibility
85.             z.object({
86.                 id: z.string(),
87.                 sessionName: z.string().optional(), // Custom session name for this event
88.                 date: z.string().optional(), // e.g., "February 27, 2026"
89.                 time: z.string().optional(), // e.g., "10:00 AM" or "10:00 AM - 11:00 AM"
90.                 timeZone: z.string().optional(), // e.g., "EST", "PST", "UTC"
91.                 room: z.string().optional(), // e.g., "ST-Laurent 6"
92.                 sessionUrl: z.string().url().optional(), // Link to the session on event website
93.             })
94.         ])).default([]),
95. 
96.         // Links
97.         website: z.string().url().optional(),
98. 
99.         // Status
100.         featured: z.boolean().default(false),
101. 
102.         // Media
103.         heroImage: z.string().optional(),
104.     }),
105. });
106. 
107. // ========================
108. // ENGAGEMENT PRESENTATIONS COLLECTION
109. // ========================
110. const engagementPresentations = defineCollection({
111.     loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.md", base: "./src/content/engagementPresentations" }),
112.     schema: z.object({
113.         title: z.string(),
114.         description: z.string(),
115.         eventSlug: z.string(),
116.         presentationSlug: z.string(),
117.         sessionSlug: z.string(),
118.         sessionTitle: z.string().optional(),
119.         date: z.coerce.date().optional(),
120.         time: z.string().optional(),
121.         timeZone: z.string().optional(),
122.         room: z.string().optional(),
123.         sessionUrl: z.string().url().optional(),
124.         thumbnail: z.string().optional(),
125.         heroImage: z.string().optional(),
126.         links: z.array(z.object({
127.             type: z.enum(['slides', 'video', 'github', 'code', 'demo', 'blog', 'download', 'documentation', 'lab', 'other']),
128.             title: z.string(),
129.             url: z.string().url(),
130.             description: z.string().optional(),
131.         })).default([]),
132.         canonicalPath: z.enum(['presentation-event', 'speaking-session']).default('presentation-event'),
133.     }),
134. });
135. 
136. // ========================
137. // MEETUP GROUPS COLLECTION
138. // ========================
139. const meetupGroups = defineCollection({
140.     loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.md", base: "./src/content/meetupGroups" }),
141.     schema: z.object({
142.         title: z.string(),
143.         slug: z.string(),
144.         description: z.string(),
145. 
146.         // Location
147.         city: z.string(),
148.         state: z.string().optional(),
149.         country: z.string(),
150. 
151.         // Links
152.         website: z.string().url().optional(),
153. 
154.         // Role
155.         role: z.string().optional(),
156. 
157.         // Status
158.         featured: z.boolean().default(false),
159. 
160.         // Media
161.         heroImage: z.string().default('/images/meetups/default-meetup.svg'),
162.     }),
163. });
164. 
165. // ========================
166. // MEETUP EVENTS COLLECTION
167. // ========================
168. const meetupEvents = defineCollection({
169.     loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.md", base: "./src/content/meetupEvents" }),
170.     schema: z.object({
171.         title: z.string(),
172.         slug: z.string().optional(),
173.         description: z.string(),
174.         shortDescription: z.string().optional(),
175.         meetupGroup: z.string(), // Reference to meetup group slug
176. 
177.         // Date and time
178.         date: z.coerce.date(),
179.         time: z.string().optional(),
180. 
181.         // Links
182.         eventUrl: z.string().url().optional(),
183. 
184.         // Speaker information
185.         speaker: z.object({
186.             name: z.string(),
187.             bio: z.string().optional(),
188.             title: z.string().optional(), // Job title/role
189.             company: z.string().optional(),
190.             photo: z.string().optional(),
191.             social: z.object({
192.                 website: z.string().url().optional(),
193.                 linkedin: z.string().url().optional(),
194.                 twitter: z.string().optional(),
195.                 github: z.string().url().optional(),
196.                 youtube: z.string().url().optional(),
197.                 sessionize: z.string().url().optional(),
198.                 bluesky: z.string().url().optional(),
199.             }).optional(),
200.         }).optional(),
201. 
202.         // Resources
203.         resources: z.array(z.object({
204.             type: z.enum(['slides', 'video', 'github', 'code', 'demo', 'blog', 'download', 'documentation', 'other']),
205.             title: z.string(),
206.             url: z.string(),
207.             description: z.string().optional(),
208.         })).optional(),
209. 
210.         // Media
211.         thumbnail: z.string().optional(),
212.         heroImage: z.string().optional(),
213. 
214.         // Location
215.         location: z.object({
216.             venue: z.string().optional(),
217.             address: z.string().optional(),
218.             city: z.string().optional(),
219.             state: z.string().optional(),
220.         }).optional(),
221. 
222.         // Status
223.         status: z.enum(['upcoming', 'past']).optional(),
224.     }),
225. });
226. 
227. // ========================
228. // BLOG COLLECTION
229. // ========================
230. const blog = defineCollection({
231.     loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.md", base: "./src/content/blog" }),
232.     schema: z.object({
233.         title: z.string(),
234.         description: z.string(),
235. 
236.         // Author
237.         author: z.string().default('Chad Green'),
238. 
239.         // Dates
240.         pubDate: z.coerce.date(),
241.         updatedDate: z.coerce.date().optional(),
242. 
243.         // Categorization
244.         category: z.enum([
245.             'Technical',
246.             'Speaking',
247.             'Community',
248.             'Career',
249.             'Tutorial',
250.             'Announcement',
251.             'Personal',
252.             'Other'
253.         ]).default('Other'),
254.         tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
255. 
256.         // Related content
257.         relatedPresentations: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
258. 
259.         // Media
260.         heroImage: z.string().default('/images/blog/default-hero.svg'),
261.         heroImageAlt: z.string().optional(),
262. 
263.         // Status
264.         draft: z.boolean().default(false),
265.         featured: z.boolean().default(false),
266. 
267.         // SEO
268.         canonicalUrl: z.string().url().optional(),
269.     }),
270. });
271. 
272. // ========================
273. // AUTHORS COLLECTION
274. // ========================
275. const authors = defineCollection({
276.     loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.md", base: "./src/content/authors" }),
277.     schema: z.object({
278.         name: z.string(),
279.         tagline: z.string().optional(),
280.         bio: z.string().optional(),
281.         shortBio: z.string().optional(),
282.         avatar: z.string().default('/images/authors/default-avatar.svg'),
283.         email: z.string().email().optional(),
284.         social: z.object({
285.             twitter: z.string().optional(),
286.             linkedin: z.string().url().optional(),
287.             github: z.string().url().optional(),
288.             youtube: z.string().url().optional(),
289.             website: z.string().url().optional(),
290.             sessionize: z.string().url().optional(),
291.         }).optional(),
292.     }),
293. });
294. 
295. // ========================
296. // SITE DATA COLLECTION
297. // ========================
298. const siteData = defineCollection({
299.     loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.md", base: "./src/content/siteData" }),
300.     schema: z.object({
301.         name: z.string(),
302.         tagline: z.string(),
303.         description: z.string(),
304.         email: z.string().email().optional(),
305.         socialLinks: z.array(z.object({
306.             platform: z.string(),
307.             url: z.string().url(),
308.             icon: z.string(),
309.         })).optional(),
310.     }),
311. });
312. 
313. export const collections = {
314.     presentations,
315.     events,
316.     engagementPresentations,
317.     meetupGroups,
318.     meetupEvents,
319.     blog,
320.     authors,
321.     siteData,
322. };
323.
```

</details>

```
26. ## About dev up
27. 
28. dev up is a premier developer conference serving the Midwest software development community. With a focus on cutting-edge technologies, software architecture, and professional development, dev up brings together developers, architects, and technology leaders for a day of intensive learning and networking.
29. 
30. The conference features expert speakers sharing practical insights on cloud architecture, microservices, security, and emerging technologies. With sessions ranging from beginner to advanced levels, dev up caters to developers at all stages of their careers.
31. 
32. ## Event Details
33. 
34. **Date:** August 16, 2024  
35. **Location:** [The Ameristar Casino Hotel](https://www.ameristar.com/st-charles), St. Charles, MO
36. 
37. ## Community Focus
38. 
39. dev up prioritizes creating an engaging environment where developers can learn from industry experts, share experiences, and build connections with fellow professionals in the development community.
40. 
41.
```

Going forward, every delivery listed in a repo's README — no matter how old — gets a full event file and engagement presentation. This aligns with what was done for Going Schema-less (#13), which included deliveries back to July 2021.

<sub>⏱️ 345m 57s</sub>

### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\repos\chadgreen.com\src\content\presentations\going-schemaless-azure-cosmosdb.md.**

C:\repos\chadgreen.com\src\content\presentations\going-schemaless-azure-cosmosdb.md

<details>
<summary>54 lines</summary>

```
1. ---
2. title: "Going Schema-less: How to migrate a relational database to a NoSQL database"
3. description: "Dive into migrating from a relational database to a NoSQL counterpart using Azure Cosmos DB—explore the benefits, challenges, and strategies for a successful schema-less transition."
4. type: "session"
5. durations: [45, 60, 75]
6. level: "intermediate"
7. tags:
8.   - "cosmosdb"
9.   - "nosql"
10.   - "azure"
11.   - "database"
12.   - "schemaless"
13.   - "data"
14.   - "dotnet"
15.   - "architecture"
16.   - "cloud"
17.   - "data-modeling"
18. heroImage: "/images/presentations/going-schemaless-azure-cosmosdb.webp"
19. status: "active"
20. featured: false
21. 
22. learningObjectives:
23.   - "Learn about the pros and cons of developing a NoSQL data model over a relational one."
24.   - "Learn how to migrate data from a relational database to a NoSQL data model."
25.   - "Learn how to develop a .NET application to use a Cosmos DB NoSQL data model."
26. 
27. resources:
28.   - type: "github"
29.     title: "GoingSchemaless Repository"
30.     url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/GoingSchemaless"
31.     description: "Slides and session materials"
32. ---
33. 
34. In the ever-evolving landscape of database management, the debate between relational and NoSQL databases remains ongoing. While relational databases have long been the stalwarts of structured data management, NoSQL databases offer a compelling alternative with their schema-less design and horizontal scalability. This presentation dives into migrating from a relational database to a NoSQL counterpart, exploring the benefits and challenges of such a transition.
35. 
36. We will unravel the intricacies of NoSQL databases, highlighting their flexibility and agility in handling diverse data structures. Through real-world examples and case studies, we will illustrate the tangible advantages that NoSQL databases offer over their relational counterparts, including improved scalability, performance, and schema flexibility.
37. 
38. Moreover, we will uncover the secret to successful migration: the strategic data allocation across multiple data sources. By embracing a polyglot persistence approach, organizations can leverage the strength of different database technologies to optimize data storage and retrieval.
39. 
40. Attendees will gain actionable insights into the migration process, from initial assessment and planning to execution and optimization. Whether contemplating a migration or seeking to optimize your existing database architecture, this session equips you with the knowledge and strategies to navigate the transition to a schema-less paradigm.
41. 
42. ### Target Audience
43. 
44. - .NET developers with relational database experience looking to explore NoSQL options
45. - Software architects evaluating Azure Cosmos DB for new or existing applications
46. - Database developers seeking practical migration strategies from SQL to NoSQL
47. - Teams considering polyglot persistence approaches for their cloud-native solutions
48. 
49. ### Prerequisites
50. 
51. - Working knowledge of relational database concepts (tables, schemas, SQL)
52. - Basic familiarity with C# and .NET development
53. - No prior Cosmos DB or NoSQL experience required
54.
```

</details>

```
22. 
23. ## About KCDC
24. 
25. KCDC (Kansas City Developer Conference) is one of the premier developer conferences in the Midwest, held annually at the Kansas City Convention Center. The conference draws thousands of attendees from across the region and beyond for multi-day programming covering cloud architecture, software development, security, DevOps, and emerging technologies.
26. 
27. KCDC is known for its high-quality speaker lineup, welcoming community atmosphere, and broad range of sessions suitable for developers at all experience levels.
28. 
29. ## Event Details
30. 
31. **Dates:** August 13–15, 2025  
32. **Location:** Kansas City Convention Center, Kansas City, MO
```

```
9. time: "3:30 PM"
10. timeZone: "CDT"
11. room: "2201"
12. links:
13.   - type: "slides"
14.     title: "Session Slides"
15.     url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/GoingSchemaless/blob/main/EventMaterials/GoingSchemaless-KCDC2025.pdf"
16. canonicalPath: "speaking-session"
17. ---
18. 
19. Thanks for attending **Going Schema-less** at **KCDC 2025**!
20. 
21. ## Going Schema-less: How to migrate a relational database to a NoSQL database
22. 
23. In the ever-evolving landscape of database management, NoSQL databases offer a compelling alternative with their schema-less design and horizontal scalability. This session dives into migrating from a relational database to a NoSQL counterpart, exploring the benefits and challenges of such a transition—including leveraging multiple data stores through a polyglot persistence approach.
24. 
25. ### Learning Objectives
26. 
27. - Learn about the pros and cons of developing a NoSQL data model over a relational one.
28. - Learn how to migrate data from a relational database to a NoSQL data model.
29. - Learn how to develop a .NET application to use a Cosmos DB NoSQL data model.
30.
```

</details>

```
23. 
24. Are you ready to advance your serverless architecture skills? Join this dynamic session to master serverless design patterns and learn to architect like a pro!
25. 
26. In this power-packed session, we will dive deep into serverless architecture, unveiling the secrets behind building scalable, resilient, cost-effective applications. From event-driven architectures to microservices and beyond, we will explore the essential design patterns that empower you to craft serverless solutions like a seasoned pro.
27. 
28. With expert guidance, you will gain invaluable insights and actionable strategies to tackle common challenges such as asynchronous workloads, workflow management, and error handling in the serverless realm. Whether you are a seasoned cloud architect or a curious newcomer, this session promises to equip you with the knowledge and tools to architect serverless applications with confidence and finesse.
29. 
30. Take advantage of this opportunity to elevate your skills and architect like a boss in serverless computing. Join us and unlock the keys to mastering serverless design patterns for unparalleled success!
31.
```

<details>
<summary>32 lines</summary>

```
1. ---
2. title: "In Defense of Microservices"
3. slug: "in-defense-of-microservices"
4. description: "Once hailed as the solution for modern applications, microservices are now criticized for complexity and operational bottlenecks. Here we will address critiques with practical solutions and showcase actual implementations. Discover why microservices still matter and how to adopt them effectively."
5. type: "session"
6. level: "intermediate"
7. durations: [75]
8. tags: ["Microservices", "Cloud Architecture", "Architecture", "Scalability", "Design Patterns"]
9. featured: false
10. heroImage: "/images/presentations/in-defense-of-microservices.webp"
11. status: "active"
12. 
13. learningObjectives:
14.   - "**Discover Why Microservices Benefits Matter:** This session will provide an overview of the key benefits of microservices, including scalability, fault isolation, and independent teams and deployments, and explain why these benefits are crucial for modern applications."
15.   - "**Lean into Microservices:** A session focused on taking typical challenges to microservices architecture as a starting point to practically show that microservices are the way to approach architecture, covering strategies, best practices, challenges, and solutions to reduce complexity, avoid operational bottlenecks, and poor performance."
16.   - "**Comparing Microservices to Other Architectural Styles:** The session will enable attendees to contrast microservices with different architectural styles, such as a modular monolith, and determine how to choose the right approach for their project and organization based on their specific needs."
17. 
18. resources:
19.   - title: "Slides"
20.     url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/InDefenseOfMicroservices/tree/main/EventMaterials"
21.     type: "slides"
22. ---
23. 
24. In recent years, the conversation around microservices as an architectural style has been highly mixed and often polarized. Microservices were hailed as the solution for modern applications, but are criticized for their complexity and operational bottlenecks. This session will:
25. 
26. - **Reaffirm the core benefits of microservices**, including scalability, fault tolerance, and independent deployments.
27. - **Address common critiques from various experts** and propose practical solutions to mitigate these issues.
28. - **Showcase successful microservice implementations** and discuss lessons learned from successes and challenges encountered in real-world projects led by the presenter.
29. - **Compare microservices with other architectural styles**, such as monoliths, n-tier architecture, and modular monoliths.
30. 
31. Join us to explore why microservices still matter and how to adopt them effectively in your organization.
32.
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>39 lines</summary>

```
1. ---
2. title: "The Dark Side of Design Patterns: What They Don't Tell You"
3. slug: "the-dark-side-of-design-patterns"
4. description: "Dive into the paradox of software design patterns! Uncover hidden complexities and pitfalls. Learn which patterns create more burden than benefit and how to mitigate their negative impacts."
5. type: "session"
6. level: "intermediate"
7. durations: [45, 60, 75]
8. tags: ["Design Patterns", "Code Quality", "Best Practices", "Complexity", "Maintainability", "Performance", "Software Engineering", "Productivity", "Scalability", "Robust Software Systems", "Unintended Consequences"]
9. featured: false
10. heroImage: "/images/presentations/the-dark-side-of-design-patterns.webp"
11. status: "active"
12. 
13. learningObjectives:
14.   - "**Identify Design Patterns That Introduce Complexity:** Recognize which patterns can inadvertently add more burden than benefit to your codebase."
15.   - "**Understand Real-World Implications:** Learn how certain design patterns lead to convoluted code, performance bottlenecks, and intricate debugging sessions."
16.   - "**Develop Mitigation Strategies:** Discover techniques to avoid the pitfalls and negative impacts of overused or misapplied design patterns."
17. 
18. resources:
19.   - title: "Repository & Slides"
20.     url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/TheDarkSideOfDesignPatterns"
21.     type: "slides"
22. ---
23. 
24. Design patterns are potent tools in software development that solve recurring problems and are essential for creating maintainable, scalable, and robust systems. However, not all design patterns are created equal. Some well-respected patterns may inadvertently lead to increased complexity, decreased maintainability, and hinder productivity.
25. 
26. Join us as we explore specific design patterns, including Factory, Observer, Singleton, and Template Method, that can cause more work than they are worth. Through real-world scenarios, we will uncover how these patterns can lead to unintended consequences such as convoluted code, performance bottlenecks, and intricate debugging sessions.
27. 
28. This presentation challenges conventional wisdom about design patterns and provides practical guidance on when to use them, when to avoid them, and how to recognize when a pattern creates more problems than it solves.
29. 
30. ## Topics Covered
31. 
32. This presentation explores the following design patterns and their potential pitfalls:
33. 
34. - **Factory Pattern:** When object creation abstraction adds unnecessary layers
35. - **Observer Pattern:** Potential over-complexity and performance implications
36. - **Singleton Pattern:** Thread safety issues and hidden dependencies
37. - **Template Method Pattern:** Tight coupling and inflexibility concerns
38. 
39.
```

</details>

```
5. type: "session"

25. 
26. In today’s fast-evolving cloud landscape, messaging patterns are the quiet enablers behind resilient, high-performance systems. This session unlocks the secrets of these paradigms, from Point-to-Point to the innovative Saga Pattern, empowering architects and developers to design systems that scale gracefully and respond dynamically. Through hands-on implementations and vivid real-world case studies, you’ll gain technical mastery and a fresh perspective on integrating effective, future-proof messaging in your cloud solutions.
27.
```

</details>

```
3. slug: "navigating-maze-architecture-decisions"
4. description: "DA guided tour of how ADRs bring structure, clarity, and long‑term value to architectural decision‑making by capturing context, rationale, and consequences with precision."
5. type: "session"

15.   - "**Craft Clear and Actionable ADRs:** Acquire practical, hands-on techniques for authoring and maintaining ADRs that document context, rationale, and trade-offs, ensuring they remain a valuable resource throughout your project’s lifecycle."

23. 
24. Clarity in articulating design choices is the bedrock of successful software architecture. This session explores the transformative power of Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) as a tool to capture the nuanced "why" behind every architectural choice, from selecting a framework to defining a core pattern. Through compelling real-world examples, we break down the essentials of ADRs and demonstrate how to document context, rationale, and consequences effectively.
25. 
26. You will walk away with actionable guidelines for authoring, maintaining, and evolving ADRs throughout your project lifecycle, providing a blueprint for clear, confident communication. Whether you’re a seasoned architect refining complex decision processes or a developer venturing into architectural design, this talk will empower you to make your design rationale accessible and impactful.
27.
```

</details>

```
1. ---
2. title: "Time Travelling Data: A Quick Overview of SQL Server Temporal Tables"
3. slug: "time-travelling-data-lightning"
4. description: "A quick, practical overview of how temporal tables bring first‑class, system‑versioned history to SQL Server and simplify your data architecture."
5. type: "lightning-talk"
6. level: "introductory"
7. durations: [20]
8. tags: ["SQL Server", "Azure SQL", "Data", "Entity Framework", ".NET", "Database", "Architecture"]
9. featured: false
10. heroImage: "/images/presentations/time-travelling-data.webp"
11. status: "active"
12. 
13. learningObjectives:
14.   - "Understand the key scenarios around the use of SQL Server temporal tables"
15.   - "Understand how to get started using temporal tables"
16.   - "Understand best practices and limitations of temporal tables"
17. 
18. resources:
19.   - title: "Slides"
20.     url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/TimeTravellingData/tree/main/Presentations"
21.     type: "slides"
22.   - title: "Demos"
23.     url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/TimeTravellingData/tree/main/Demos"
24.     type: "code"
25. ---
26. 
27. Often, customers will ask what the data looked like on a particular date, and you might have built complex triggers and procedures to track that history. But SQL Server and Azure SQL already have a built-in solution, and it's straightforward to use. In this quick overview, you will learn what Temporal Tables are, key scenarios for their use, and how to use built-in query syntax to retrieve the values of database records at a point in time. We will also look at how Entity Framework Core makes this even easier now with Temporal Table support.
28.
```

</details>

```
23. 
24. Join us on a journey through the Viking village of Cloudheim to explore the transformative power of serverless computing and API design architectures. Led by Eirik the Enlightened and Bjorn the Bold, we will illustrate how these technologies enable agility, scalability, and cost-effectiveness.
25. 
26. Discover how serverless computing reduces operational overhead and accelerates time-to-market. Learn about the key components and benefits of serverless architectures, including event-driven architectures and seamless scalability.
27. 
28. Understand the role of API design in modern software development, focusing on modularity, reusability, and interoperability. We will cover API design principles, the importance of API gateways, and real-world examples showcasing their transformative potential.
29. 
30. We will also address challenges such as cold start latency, vendor lock-in, security, and monitoring. Gain practical insights and anticipate future trends in serverless computing and API design.
31.
```

Background agent "Audit all presentation repos" (general-purpose) completed

<details>
<summary>Detail</summary>


You are working on the chadgreen.com Astro 5 static site (C:\repos\chadgreen.com) on branch `issue/22-audit-all-presentations`. Your task is to audit ALL TaleLearnCode presentation repos and ensure every presentation has accurate content, hero images, event pages, and engagement presentations on the site.

## CRITICAL RULES

1. **NEVER fabricate learning objectives** — copy them exactly from the repo README
2. **Do NOT add NDC Oslo or Refactor events** (Chad has never spoken there)
3. **Do NOT add canceled events** (skip rows marked "Canceled" in README tables)
4. **ALL other deliveries MUST be included** regardless of age (2019, 2020, 2021, etc.)
5. **Private repo = source of truth** when it exists; use public repo as fallback
6. **Do NOT add a `slug` field to presentation frontmatter** (causes duplicate ID warnings — filename stem is already the ID)
7. **Build must pass** before committing

## FILE FORMATS

### Presentation: `src/content/presentations/{slug}.md`
```markdown
---
title: "Full Presentation Title"
description: "One-sentence description (from repo short abstract or elevator pitch)"
type: "session"  # session | workshop | lightning-talk | keynote | panel | webinar
durations: [45, 60, 75]  # array of supported minute lengths
level: "intermediate"  # introductory | intermediate | advanced | all
tags:
  - "tag1"
  - "tag2"
heroImage: "/images/presentations/{slug}.webp"
status: "active"  # active | retired | in-development
featured: false

learningObjectives:
  - "Exact LO text from README."
  - "Exact LO text from README."

resources:
  - type: "github"
    title: "RepoName Repository"
    url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/RepoName"
    description: "Slides and session materials"
---

Full abstract text from repo (NOT the short abstract/elevator pitch).

### Target Audience

- bullet points describing who this is for

### Prerequisites

- bullet points of what attendees should know
```

### Event: `src/content/events/{event-slug}.md`
```markdown
---
title: "Conference Name Year"
slug: "{event-slug}"
description: "Brief description of the conference."
startDate: YYYY-MM-DD
endDate: YYYY-MM-DD
location:
  venue: "Venue Name"
  city: "City"
  state: "State"  # omit for international
  country: "USA"
website: "https://conference.com"
featured: false
heroImage: "/images/events/{event-slug}.webp"
presentations:
  - id: "presentation-slug"
    date: "Month Day, Year"
    time: "10:00 AM"
    timeZone: "CDT"
    room: "Room Name"
    sessionUrl: "https://..."  # optional
---

## About Conference Name

Brief description.

## Event Details

**Dates:** Month Day–Day, Year  
**Location:** Venue, City, State
```

### Engagement Presentation: `src/content/engagementPresentations/{event-slug}-{presentation-slug}.md`
```markdown
---
title: "Presentation Title"
description: "Session details and resources for Event Name: Presentation Title."
eventSlug: "event-slug"
presentationSlug: "presentation-slug"
sessionSlug: "presentation-slug"
sessionTitle: "Presentation Title"
date: YYYY-MM-DD
time: "10:00 AM"
timeZone: "CDT"
room: "Room Name"
sessionUrl: "https://..."  # optional
links:
  - type: "slides"
    title: "Session Slides"
    url: "https://..."
canonicalPath: "speaking-session"
---

Thanks for attending **Presentation Title** at **Event Name Year**!

## Presentation Title

[One paragraph summary from abstract]

### Learning Objectives

- Exact LO 1
- Exact LO 2
```

## THUMBNAIL WORKFLOW

For each new presentation:
1. Try: `Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/TaleLearnCode/{RepoName}/main/thumbnail.jpg" -OutFile "C:\Temp\{slug}.jpg"`
2. If 404, try: `thumbnail.png`, `Thumbnail.jpg`, `Thumbnail.png`
3. Convert: `node -e "const sharp = require('./node_modules/sharp'); sharp('C:/Temp/{slug}.jpg').webp({quality:85}).toFile('./public/images/presentations/{slug}.webp').then(i=>console.log(i)).catch(e=>console.error(e));"`
4. If no thumbnail found, use the existing default or skip heroImage field

```
18. 
19. Thanks for attending **Architect Like a Boss** at **Nebraska.Code() 2024**!
20. 
21. ## Architect Like a Boss: Mastering Serverless Design Patterns
22. 
23. Are you ready to advance your serverless architecture skills? Join this dynamic session where you will master serverless design patterns and learn to architect like a boss! We dive deep into serverless architecture, unveiling the secrets behind building scalable, resilient, cost-effective applications. From event-driven architectures to microservices and beyond, we explore the essential design patterns that empower you to craft serverless solutions like a seasoned pro.
24. 
25. ### Learning Objectives
26. 
27. - **Master Key Serverless Design Patterns:** Understand and apply essential patterns for scalable, resilient, cost-effective serverless solutions.
28. - **Implement Best Practices:** Learn techniques for asynchronous workloads, workflow management, and error handling in serverless architecture.
29. - **Gain Confidence in Serverless Design:** Acquire the skills and knowledge to tackle complex challenges and make informed architectural decisions.
30.
```

</details>

We dive deep into serverless architecture, unveiling the secrets behind building scalable, resilient, cost-effective applications. From event-driven architectures to microservices and beyond, we explore the essential design patterns that empower you to craft serverless solutions like a seasoned pro.
```
24.

28. - **Implement Best Practices:** Learn techniques for asynchronous workloads, workflow management, and error handling in serverless architecture.
```

```
18. 
19. Thanks for attending **Time Travelling Data** at **TechBash 2023**!
20. 
21. ## Time Travelling Data: A Quick Overview of SQL Server Temporal Tables
22. 
23. Often, customers will ask what the data looked like on a particular date, and you might have built complex triggers and procedures to track that history. But SQL Server and Azure SQL already have a built-in solution, and it's straightforward to use. In this session, you will learn what Temporal Tables are, key scenarios for their use, and how to use built-in query syntax to retrieve the values of database records at a point in time. We will also look at how Entity Framework Core makes this even easier now with Temporal Table support.
24. 
25. ### Learning Objectives
26. 
27. - Understand the key scenarios around the use of SQL Server temporal tables
28. - Understand how to get started using temporal tables
29. - Understand best practices and limitations of temporal tables
30.
```

</details>

<summary>30 lines</summary>

```
successfully downloaded text file (SHA: 7d2ce556dcf81e279bd7234118be44f716cfc6c8)![Time Travelling Data: SQL Server Temporal Tables](Thumbnail.jpg)

## Abstract
Have you built complicated triggers and procedures to track the history of data in your databases? What if SQL Server or Azure SQL could take care of all that for you and you just had to change a couple of settings? Starting with SQL Server 2016, there is support for system-versioned temporal tables as a database feature that brings built-in support for providing information about data stored in a table at any point in time rather than only the data that is correct as the current moment time. During this session, Chad will explain the key scenarios around the use of Temporal Tables, how system-time works, how to get started, and finish up with a demo to show you Temporal Tables in action, including the easy-to-use T-SQL syntax to implement all of the Temporal goodness. We will also look at how Entity Framework Core 6 now lets us do all this directly in Entity Framework.

## Short Abstract
During this session, Chad will explain the key scenarios around the use of Temporal Tables, how system-time works, how to get started, and finish up with a demo to show you Temporal Tables in action, including the easy-to-use T-SQL syntax to implement all of the Temporal goodness. We will also look at how Entity Framework Core 6 now lets us do all this directly in Entity Framework.

# Type
* 45/60/75-minute session

## Tags
* .NET
* Architecture
* C#
* Data
* Database
* Entity Framework
* SQL
* SQL Server

## Learning Objectives
* Understand the key scenarios around the use of SQL Server temporal tables
* Understand how to get started using temporal tables
* Understand best practices and limitations of temporal tables

## Presentations

| Event | Location | Date | Time | Room | Downloads |
|-------|:--------:|-----:|-----:|-----:|----------:|
| [Visual Studio Live!](https://vslive.com/events/las-vegas-2026/sessions/wednesday/w09-ff-data.aspx) | Las Vegas, NV | 2026-03-18 | 13:30 PDT | W09 | [Slides](EventMaterials/TimeTravellingData_VSLiveVegas2026.pdf) |
| [TechBash](https://techbash.com/) | Pocono Manor, PA | 2023-11-08 | 13:30 EST | Aloeswood | [Slides](Presentations/TimeTravellingData_TechBash2023.pdf) |
| [DevSpace](https://www.devspaceconf.com/sessions.html?id=937) | Huntsville, AL | 2023-10-24 | 14:30 CDT | Ballroom 4 | [Slides](Presentations/TimeTravellingData_DevSpace2023.pdf) |
| [Prairie Dev Con Winnipeg](https://www.prairiedevcon.com/winnipeg.html) | Winnipeg, MB | 2022-11-08 | 14:15 CST | A3 | [Slides](Presentations/TimeTravellingData_PDCWinnipeg.pdf) |
| [KCDC](https://www.kcdc.info/agenda) | Kansas City, MO | 2022-08-09 | 15:30 CDT | 2201 | [Slides](Presentations/TimeTravellingData_KCDC2022.pdf) |
```

<details>
<summary>27 lines</summary>

```
1. ---
2. title: "Time Travelling Data: A Quick Overview of SQL Server Temporal Tables"
3. description: "A quick, practical overview of how temporal tables bring first‑class, system‑versioned history to SQL Server and simplify your data architecture."
4. type: "session"
5. level: "introductory"
6. durations: [45, 60, 75]
7. tags: ["SQL Server", "Azure SQL", "Data", "Entity Framework", ".NET", "Database", "Architecture"]
8. featured: false
9. heroImage: "/images/presentations/time-travelling-data.webp"
10. status: "active"
11. 
12. learningObjectives:
13.   - "Understand the key scenarios around the use of SQL Server temporal tables"
14.   - "Understand how to get started using temporal tables"
15.   - "Understand best practices and limitations of temporal tables"
16. 
17. resources:
18.   - title: "Slides"
19.     url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/TimeTravellingData/tree/main/Presentations"
20.     type: "slides"
21.   - title: "Demos"
22.     url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/TimeTravellingData/tree/main/Demos"
23.     type: "code"
24. ---
25. 
26. Often, customers will ask what the data looked like on a particular date, and you might have built complex triggers and procedures to track that history. But SQL Server and Azure SQL already have a built-in solution, and it's straightforward to use. In this quick overview, you will learn what Temporal Tables are, key scenarios for their use, and how to use built-in query syntax to retrieve the values of database records at a point in time. We will also look at how Entity Framework Core makes this even easier now with Temporal Table support.
27.
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>39 lines</summary>

```
1. ---
2. title: "Time Travelling Data: A Quick Overview of SQL Server Temporal Tables"
3. description: "Session details and resources for Visual Studio Live! Las Vegas 2026: Fast Focus: Time Travelling Data."
4. eventSlug: "vslive-las-vegas-2026"
5. presentationSlug: "time-travelling-data-lightning"
6. sessionSlug: "time-travelling-data"
7. sessionTitle: "Fast Focus: Time Travelling Data"
8. date: 2026-03-18
9. time: "1:30 PM"
10. timeZone: "PST"
11. room: "TBA"
12. sessionUrl: "https://vslive.com/events/las-vegas-2026/sessions/wednesday/w09-ff-data.aspx"
13. canonicalPath: "speaking-session"
14. ---
15. Thanks for joining this session at **Visual Studio Live! Las Vegas 2026**.
16. 
17. ## Fast Focus: Time Travelling Data
18. 
19. Many teams need to answer questions like “what did this record look like last week?” and often rely on custom triggers and history procedures to get there. This session shows how SQL Server and Azure SQL already provide built-in temporal table capabilities to solve this problem more cleanly. You’ll see key use cases, point-in-time query syntax, and how Entity Framework Core support makes temporal querying easier in modern .NET applications.
20. 
21. ### What This Session Covers
22. 
23. This lightning-style session focuses on practical, built-in options for historical data analysis without complex custom auditing infrastructure.
24. 
25. You’ll walk through:
26. 
27. - What temporal tables are and the scenarios where they add immediate value.
28. - How to get started with temporal tables in SQL Server and Azure SQL.
29. - Point-in-time querying patterns to retrieve record state at a specific date and time.
30. - How Entity Framework Core streamlines temporal table access in application code.
31. 
32. ### Practical Outcomes
33. 
34. By the end of the session, you should be able to:
35. 
36. - Identify business scenarios where temporal tables are the right fit.
37. - Implement a starter temporal table strategy and query historical data effectively.
38. - Recognize best practices and limitations before adopting temporal tables at scale.
39.
```

</details>


---

### Presentation: `src/content/presentations/{slug}.md`
```markdown
---
title: "Full Title"
description: "One-sentence description"
type: "session"  # session | workshop | lightning-talk | keynote | panel | webinar
durations: [45, 60, 75]
level: "intermediate"  # introductory | intermediate | advanced | all
tags:
  - "Tag1"
heroImage: "/images/presentations/{slug}.webp"
status: "active"  # active | retired | in-development
featured: false
learningObjectives:
  - "Exact LO from README."
resources:
  - type: "github"
    title: "RepoName Repository"
    url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/RepoName"
    description: "Slides and session materials"
---

Full abstract text from repo.

### Target Audience
- bullet points

### Prerequisites

```

### Event: `src/content/events/{event-slug}.md`
```markdown
---
title: "Conference Name Year"
slug: "{event-slug}"
description: "Brief description."
startDate: YYYY-MM-DD
endDate: YYYY-MM-DD
location:
  venue: "Venue"

    room: "Room Name"
---
```

When adding a presentation to an existing event file, add it to the `presentations:` array. Check what engagement presentations already exist before creating new ones.

## WORKFLOW

1. Use github-mcp-server-get_file_contents to read READMEs from TaleLearnCode repos
2. Extract: title, abstract, short abstract, type, durations, learning objectives, tags, delivery history
3. Check existing files with glob/view tools
4. Create/update files as needed
5. Build with `npm run build` to verify (exit code 0 = success; warnings are OK)
6. Commit after 4-5 presentations

## COMMIT FORMAT
```
Add/verify presentations: {list}

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>
```

## FINAL STEP
After all work is done: `git push -u origin issue/22-audit-all-presentations`


</details>

```
1. ---
2. title: "Generative AI for .NET Developers"
3. description: "Live presentation at Louisville .NET Meetup on generative AI development with .NET and Azure OpenAI, featuring live demos of AI building AI apps."
4. eventSlug: "louisville-dotnet-meetup-2024-08-22"
5. presentationSlug: "generative-ai-dotnet-developers"
6. sessionSlug: "louisville-dotnet-2024-08-22-generative-ai"
7. sessionTitle: "Generative AI for .NET Developers"
8. date: 2024-08-22
9. time: "6:30 PM"
10. timeZone: "EDT"
11. room: "Slingshot"
12. canonicalPath: "speaking-session"
13. links:
14.   - type: "github"
15.     title: "GenerativeAIForDotNetDevelopers-private"
16.     url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/GenerativeAIForDotNetDevelopers-private"
17.     description: "Presentation source materials and demo code"
18. ---
19. 
20. ## Generative AI for .NET Developers
21. 
22. Thanks for joining this session at the **Louisville .NET Meetup** on August 22, 2024!
23. 
24. ### Session Overview
25. 
26. In this hands-on session, you will discover how to use .NET, C#, and Azure to create amazing applications with OpenAI and other generative AI technologies. You will learn the fundamental concepts of this latest wave of AI, the benefits and challenges of using .NET with it, and the best practices and tips for developing intelligent apps.
27. 
28. ### Key Highlights
29. 
30. - **Live Demo:** See how AI can build AI apps using .NET and OpenAI
31. - **Practical Integration:** Learn how to integrate Azure OpenAI with your .NET applications
32. - **Best Practices:** Discover tips and tricks for developing with generative AI
33. - **Real-World Applications:** Understand when and how to use ChatGPT and large language models in your projects
34. 
35. ### Learning Objectives
36. 
37. - Understand the fundamental concepts of generative AI and its applications
38. - Learn how to integrate .NET with Azure OpenAI to create intelligent applications
39. - Explore the benefits and challenges of using .NET with large language models like ChatGPT
40. - Discover best practices and tips for developing intelligent apps with generative AI
41. 
42. ### Technologies Covered
43. 
44. - .NET and C#
45. - Azure OpenAI
46. - ChatGPT and large language models
47. - Generative AI best practices
48.
```

</details>

## CRITICAL RULES
1. **NEVER fabricate learning objectives** — copy them EXACTLY from the repo README
2. **Do NOT add NDC Oslo or Refactor events**
3. **Do NOT add canceled events**
4. **ALL other deliveries MUST be included** regardless of age
5. **Private repo = source of truth**; fall back to public
6. **Do NOT add a `slug` field to presentation frontmatter**
7. **Build must pass** — run `npm run build` to verify (exit 0 = success, warnings OK)
8. **Commit after every 3-4 repos** with message format: `Add presentations: {list}\n\nCo-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>`

## FILE FORMATS

### Presentation: `src/content/presentations/{slug}.md`
```
---
title: "Full Title"
description: "One-sentence description"
type: "session"  # session|workshop|lightning-talk|keynote|panel|webinar
durations: [45, 60, 75]
level: "intermediate"  # introductory|intermediate|advanced|all
tags:
  - "Tag1"
heroImage: "/images/presentations/{slug}.webp"
status: "active"
featured: false
learningObjectives:
  - "Exact LO from README."
resources:
  - type: "github"
    title: "RepoName Repository"
    url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/RepoName"
    description: "Slides and session materials"
---

Full abstract text.

### Target Audience
- bullets

### Prerequisites
- bullets
```

### Event: `src/content/events/{slug}.md`
```
---
title: "Conference Name Year"
slug: "{slug}"
description: "Brief description."
startDate: YYYY-MM-DD
endDate: YYYY-MM-DD
location:
  venue: "Venue"
  city: "City"
  state: "State"
  country: "USA"
website: "https://..."
featured: false
heroImage: "/images/events/default-event.webp"
presentations:
  - id: "presentation-slug"
    date: "Month Day, Year"
    time: "10:00 AM"
    timeZone: "CDT"
    room: "Room Name"
---

## About Conference Name

Brief description.
```

### Engagement: `src/content/engagementPresentations/{event-slug}-{presentation-slug}.md`

```
timeZone: "CDT"
room: "Room Name"

    url: "https://..."
canonicalPath: "speaking-session"
---

## Title

[Abstract paragraph]

### Learning Objectives

```

## PROCESS FOR EACH REPO
1. Read private README (try `{Repo}-private`), fall back to public
2. Extract: title, abstract, short abstract, type, durations, learning objectives, tags, delivery table
3. Download & convert thumbnail
4. Create/update `src/content/presentations/{slug}.md`
5. For each delivery row (skip Canceled): check if event file exists, create if missing; check if engagement file exists, create if missing
6. Build to verify, commit

## IMPORTANT NOTES
- Event slug format: `{conference-name}-{YYYY}` (e.g., `kcdc-2023`, `beer-city-code-2022`)
- For user group/meetup events with a specific date: `{group-name}-{YYYY-MM-DD}` 
- The engagementPresentations slug field `sessionSlug` should match `presentationSlug`
- Check `src/content/engagementPresentations/` with glob before creating files
- When adding to existing event, add entry to `presentations:` array; also verify the engagement file doesn't already exist

## FILE FORMATS

### Presentation: `src/content/presentations/{slug}.md`
```
---
title: "Full Title"
description: "One-sentence description"
type: "session"
durations: [45, 60, 75]
level: "intermediate"
tags:
  - "Tag1"
heroImage: "/images/presentations/{slug}.webp"

  - "Exact LO from README."

  - type: "github"
    title: "RepoName Repository"
    url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/RepoName"
    description: "Slides and session materials"
---
Full abstract text.

### Target Audience
- bullets

### Prerequisites
- bullets
```

```
---
title: "Conference Name Year"
slug: "{slug}"
description: "Brief description."
startDate: YYYY-MM-DD
endDate: YYYY-MM-DD

  venue: "Venue"
  city: "City"
  state: "State"
  country: "USA"

featured: false

---

Brief description.
```

```
description: "Session details and resources for Event Name: Presentation Title."
eventSlug: "event-slug"
```

## CRITICAL RULES
1. **NEVER fabricate learning objectives** — copy EXACTLY from README
2. **Do NOT add NDC Oslo or Refactor events**
3. **Do NOT add canceled events** (skip rows with "Canceled")
4. **ALL other deliveries MUST be included** regardless of age
5. **Do NOT add a `slug` field to presentation frontmatter**
6. **Build must pass** — `npm run build` (exit 0 = success, warnings OK)
7. **Commit after every 3-4 repos**

## FILE FORMATS

### Presentation: `src/content/presentations/{slug}.md`
```
---
title: "Full Title"
description: "One-sentence description"
type: "session"
durations: [45, 60, 75]
level: "intermediate"
tags:
  - "Tag1"
heroImage: "/images/presentations/{slug}.webp"
status: "active"
featured: false
learningObjectives:
  - "Exact LO from README."
resources:
  - type: "github"
    title: "RepoName Repository"
    url: "https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/RepoName"
    description: "Slides and session materials"
---
Full abstract text.

### Target Audience
- bullets

### Prerequisites
- bullets
```

### Event: `src/content/events/{slug}.md`
```
---
title: "Conference Name Year"
slug: "{slug}"
description: "Brief description."
startDate: YYYY-MM-DD
endDate: YYYY-MM-DD
location:
  venue: "Venue"
  city: "City"
  state: "State"
  country: "USA"
website: "https://..."
featured: false
heroImage: "/images/events/default-event.webp"
presentations:
  - id: "presentation-slug"
    date: "Month Day, Year"
    time: "10:00 AM"
    timeZone: "CDT"
    room: "Room Name"
---

## About Conference Name
Brief description.
```

```
---
title: "Presentation Title"
description: "Session details and resources for Event Name: Presentation Title."
eventSlug: "event-slug"
presentationSlug: "presentation-slug"
sessionSlug: "presentation-slug"
sessionTitle: "Presentation Title"
date: YYYY-MM-DD
time: "10:00 AM"
timeZone: "CDT"
room: "Room Name"
links:
  - type: "slides"
    title: "Session Slides"
    url: "https://..."
canonicalPath: "speaking-session"
---

Thanks for attending **Title** at **Event Year**!

## Title

[Abstract paragraph]

### Learning Objectives
- Exact LO 1
```

### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\repos\chadgreen.com/src/pages/presentations/index.astro.**

C:\repos\chadgreen.com/src/pages/presentations/index.astro

<details>
<summary>183 lines</summary>

```
1. ---
2. import BaseLayout from '../../layouts/BaseLayout.astro';
3. import PageHeader from '../../components/ui/PageHeader.astro';
4. import PresentationCard from '../../components/cards/PresentationCard.astro';
5. import { getCollection } from 'astro:content';
6. 
7. // Get all active presentations
8. let presentations: any[] = [];
9. try {
10.     const allPresentations = await getCollection('presentations');
11.     presentations = allPresentations
12.         .sort((a, b) => {
13.             // Featured first, then by creation date
14.             if (a.data.featured && !b.data.featured) return -1;
15.             if (!a.data.featured && b.data.featured) return 1;
16.             const dateA = a.data.createdDate?.getTime() || 0;
17.             const dateB = b.data.createdDate?.getTime() || 0;
18.             return dateB - dateA;
19.         });
20. } catch (e) {
21.     // Collection might not exist yet
22. }
23. 
24. // Group by type for filtering
25. const presentationTypes = [...new Set(presentations.map(p => p.data.type))];
26. ---
27. 
28. <BaseLayout 
29.     title="Presentations" 
30.     description="Browse all presentations by Chad Green on Azure, .NET, DevOps, and more."
31. >
32.     <PageHeader title="Presentations">
33.         <p>Explore my collection of talks, workshops, and presentations</p>
34.     </PageHeader>
35. 
36.     <section class="section">
37.         <div class="container">
38.             <!-- Filter Controls -->
39.             {presentationTypes.length > 1 && (
40.                 <div class="filter-controls">
41.                     <button class="filter-btn active" data-filter="all">All</button>
42.                     {presentationTypes.map((type) => (
43.                         <button class="filter-btn" data-filter={type}>
44.                             {type.replace('-', ' ').replace(/\b\w/g, l => l.toUpperCase())}
45.                         </button>
46.                     ))}
47.                 </div>
48.             )}
49. 
50.             {presentations.length > 0 ? (
51.                 <div class="presentations-grid grid grid-3" id="presentations-grid">
52.                     {presentations.map((presentation) => (
53.                         <div class="presentation-item" data-type={presentation.data.type}>
54.                             <PresentationCard
55.                                 title={presentation.data.title}
56.                                 description={presentation.data.description}
57.                                 slug={presentation.id}
58.                                 thumbnail={presentation.data.heroImage}
59.                                 presentationType={presentation.data.type}
60.                                 tags={presentation.data.tags}
61.                                 featured={presentation.data.featured}
62.                             />
63.                         </div>
64.                     ))}
65.                 </div>
66.             ) : (
67.                 <div class="empty-state">
68.                     <h3>No Presentations Yet</h3>
69.                     <p>Check back soon for my collection of presentations.</p>
70.                 </div>
71.             )}
72.         </div>
73.     </section>
74. 
75.     <!-- Book CTA -->
76.     <section class="book-cta">
77.         <div class="container">
78.             <div class="cta-content">
79.                 <p>
80.                     <strong>Interested in one of these presentations?</strong><br>
81.                     Email <a href="mailto:chadgreen@chadgreen.com">chadgreen@chadgreen.com</a> to have Chad present at your event.
82.                 </p>
83.             </div>
84.         </div>
85.     </section>
86. </BaseLayout>
87. 
88. <style>
89.     .filter-controls {
90.         display: flex;
91.         flex-wrap: wrap;
92.         gap: var(--spacing-2);
93.         margin-bottom: var(--spacing-8);
94.         justify-content: center;
95.     }
96. 
97.     .filter-btn {
98.         padding: var(--spacing-2) var(--spacing-4);
99.         background-color: var(--color-bg-secondary);
100.         border: 1px solid var(--color-border);
101.         border-radius: var(--radius-full);
102.         font-size: var(--font-size-sm);
103.         font-weight: var(--font-weight-medium);
104.         color: var(--color-text-secondary);
105.         cursor: pointer;
106.         transition: all var(--transition-fast);
107.     }
108. 
109.     .filter-btn:hover {
110.         border-color: var(--color-primary);
111.         color: var(--color-primary);
112.     }
113. 
114.     .filter-btn.active {
115.         background-color: var(--color-primary);
116.         border-color: var(--color-primary);
117.         color: var(--color-text-inverse);
118.     }
119. 
120.     .presentation-item {
121.         transition: opacity var(--transition-fast);
122.     }
123. 
124.     .presentation-item.hidden {
125.         display: none;
126.     }
127. 
128.     .empty-state {
129.         text-align: center;
130.         padding: var(--spacing-16);
131.         background-color: var(--color-bg-secondary);
132.         border-radius: var(--radius-lg);
133.     }
134. 
135.     .empty-state h3 {
136.         margin-bottom: var(--spacing-2);
137.     }
138. 
139.     .empty-state p {
140.         color: var(--color-text-secondary);
141.         margin-bottom: 0;
142.     }
143. 
144.     .book-cta {
145.         background-color: var(--color-primary-bg);
146.         padding: var(--spacing-8) 0;
147.     }
148. 
149.     .book-cta .cta-content {
150.         text-align: center;
151.     }
152. 
153.     .book-cta a {
154.         color: var(--color-primary);
155.         font-weight: var(--font-weight-semibold);
156.     }
157. </style>
158. 
159. <script>
160.     const filterBtns = document.querySelectorAll('.filter-btn');
161.     const items = document.querySelectorAll('.presentation-item');
162. 
163.     filterBtns.forEach(btn => {
164.         btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
165.             const filter = (btn as HTMLElement).dataset.filter;
166. 
167.             // Update active state
168.             filterBtns.forEach(b => b.classList.remove('active'));
169.             btn.classList.add('active');
170. 
171.             // Filter items
172.             items.forEach(item => {
173.                 const type = (item as HTMLElement).dataset.type;
174.                 if (filter === 'all' || type === filter) {
175.                     item.classList.remove('hidden');
176.                 } else {
177.                     item.classList.add('hidden');
178.                 }
179.             });
180.         });
181.     });
182. </script>
183.
```

```
1. ---
2. import BaseLayout from '../../layouts/BaseLayout.astro';
3. import Badge from '../../components/ui/Badge.astro';
4. import Button from '../../components/ui/Button.astro';
5. import TabNav from '../../components/ui/TabNav.astro';
6. import TabSyncScript from '../../components/ui/TabSyncScript.astro';
7. import EventCard from '../../components/cards/EventCard.astro';
8. import { getCollection, render } from 'astro:content';
9. import { marked } from 'marked';
10. 
11. export async function getStaticPaths() {
12.     const presentations = await getCollection('presentations');
13.     return presentations.map((presentation) => ({
14.         params: { slug: presentation.id },
15.         props: { presentation },
16.     }));
17. }
18. 
19. const { presentation } = Astro.props;
20. const { Content } = await render(presentation);
21. 
22. // Get related presentations
23. let relatedPresentations: any[] = [];
24. if (presentation.data.relatedPresentations?.length) {
25.     const allPresentations = await getCollection('presentations');
26.     relatedPresentations = allPresentations.filter(p => 
27.         presentation.data.relatedPresentations.includes(p.id)
28.     );
29. }
30. 
31. // Get events where this presentation was given
32. let presentationEvents: any[] = [];
33. try {
34.     const allEvents = await getCollection('events');
35.     presentationEvents = allEvents
36.         .filter(e => {
37.             if (!e.data.presentations) return false;
38.             // Handle both string format and object format
39.             return e.data.presentations.some((p: any) => {
40.                 const presentationId = typeof p === 'string' ? p : p.id;
41.                 return presentationId === presentation.id;
42.             });
43.         })
44.         .sort((a, b) => b.data.startDate.getTime() - a.data.startDate.getTime());
45. } catch (e) {
46.     // Collection might not exist yet
47. }
48. 
49. const tabs = [
50.     { id: 'abstract', label: 'Abstract' },
51.     { id: 'objectives', label: 'Learning Objectives' },
52.     { id: 'events', label: `Events (${presentationEvents.length})` },
53.     { id: 'resources', label: 'Resources' },
54. ];
55. 
56. // Get current tab from URL
57. const currentTab = Astro.url.searchParams.get('tab') || 'abstract';
58. 
59. const typeLabels: Record<string, string> = {
60.     'session': 'Session',
61.     'workshop': 'Workshop',
62.     'lightning-talk': 'Lightning Talk',
63.     'keynote': 'Keynote',
64.     'panel': 'Panel',
65.     'webinar': 'Webinar',
66. };
67. 
68. const statusLabels: Record<string, string> = {
69.     'active': 'Active',
70.     'retired': 'Retired',
71.     'in-development': 'In Development',
72. };
73. 
74. const statusVariants: Record<string, 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'outline' | 'success' | 'warning' | 'danger'> = {
75.     'active': 'success',
76.     'retired': 'outline',
77.     'in-development': 'warning',
78. };
79. ---
80. 
81. <BaseLayout 
82.     title={presentation.data.title}
83.     description={presentation.data.description}
84.     ogImage={presentation.data.heroImage}
85. >
86.     <!-- Presentation Header -->
87.     <section class="presentation-header">
88.         <div class="container">
89.             <div class="header-content">
90.                 <div class="header-image">
91.                     <img 
92.                         src={presentation.data.heroImage || '/images/presentations/default-thumbnail.svg'} 
93.                         alt="" 
94.                         loading="eager"
95.                     />
96.                 </div>
97.                 <div class="header-info">
98.                     <div class="header-meta">
99.                         <Badge variant="primary">{typeLabels[presentation.data.type] || presentation.data.type}</Badge>
100.                         {presentation.data.level && <Badge variant="secondary">{presentation.data.level}</Badge>}
101.                         <Badge variant={statusVariants[presentation.data.status]}>{statusLabels[presentation.data.status]}</Badge>
102.                         {presentation.data.durations && presentation.data.durations.length > 0 && (
103.                             <span class="duration">⏱ {presentation.data.durations.join(' / ')} min</span>
104.                         )}
105.                     </div>
106.                     
107.                     <h1 class="presentation-title">{presentation.data.title}</h1>
108.                     
109.                     <p class="presentation-description">{presentation.data.description}</p>
110.                     
111.                     {presentation.data.tags?.length > 0 && (
112.                         <div class="presentation-tags">
113.                             {presentation.data.tags.map((tag: string) => (
114.                                 <a href={`/presentations/tags/${encodeURIComponent(tag)}`} class="tag-link">
115.                                     <Badge variant="outline" size="sm">{tag}</Badge>
116.                                 </a>
117.                             ))}
118.                         </div>
119.                     )}
120. 
121.                     {relatedPresentations.length > 0 && (
122.                         <div class="related-presentations">
123.                             <span class="related-label">Related:</span>
124.                             {relatedPresentations.map((rp) => (
125.                                 <a href={`/presentations/${rp.id}`} class="related-link">{rp.data.shortTitle || rp.data.title}</a>
126.                             ))}
127.                         </div>
128.                     )}
129.                 </div>
130.             </div>
131.         </div>
132.     </section>
133. 
134.     <!-- Tab Navigation -->
135.     <div class="tabs-container">
136.         <div class="container">
137.             <TabNav 
138.                 tabs={tabs.map(t => ({ ...t, href: `?tab=${t.id}` }))} 
139.                 activeTab={currentTab} 
140.             />
141.         </div>
142.     </div>
143. 
144.     <!-- Tab Content -->
145.     <section class="section tab-content">
146.         <div class="container">
147.             <!-- Abstract Tab -->
148.             <div class="tab-panel" data-tab="abstract" style={currentTab !== 'abstract' ? 'display: none;' : ''}>
149.                 <div class="prose">
150.                     {presentation.data.abstract ? (
151.                         <div set:html={presentation.data.abstract} />
152.                     ) : (
153.                         <Content />
154.                     )}
155.                 </div>
156.             </div>
157. 
158.             <!-- Learning Objectives Tab -->
159.             <div class="tab-panel" data-tab="objectives" style={currentTab !== 'objectives' ? 'display: none;' : ''}>
160.                 {presentation.data.learningObjectives?.length > 0 ? (
161.                     <div class="objectives-list">
162.                         <h2>What You'll Learn</h2>
163.                         <ul>
164.                             {presentation.data.learningObjectives.map((objective: string, index: number) => (
165.                                 <li>
166.                                     <span class="objective-number">{index + 1}</span>
167.                                     <span class="objective-text" set:html={marked.parseInline(objective)} />
168.                                 </li>
169.                             ))}
170.                         </ul>
171.                     </div>
172.                 ) : (
173.                     <div class="empty-state">
174.                         <p>Learning objectives coming soon.</p>
175.                     </div>
176.                 )}
177.             </div>
178. 
179.             <!-- Events Tab -->
180.             <div class="tab-panel" data-tab="events" style={currentTab !== 'events' ? 'display: none;' : ''}>
181.                 {presentationEvents.length > 0 ? (
182.                     <div class="events-list">
183.                         <h2>Where I've Presented This</h2>
184.                         <div class="grid grid-3">
185.                             {presentationEvents.map((event) => (
186.                                 <EventCard
187.                                     title={event.data.title}
188.                                     slug={event.id}
189.                                     startDate={event.data.startDate}
190.                                     endDate={event.data.endDate}
191.                                     location={event.data.location}
192.                                     heroImage={event.data.heroImage}
193.                                     logo={event.data.logo}
194.                                     thumbnail={event.data.thumbnail}
195.                                     eventType={event.data.eventType}
196.                                     status={event.data.status}
197.                                     eventUrl={event.data.eventUrl}
198.                                     slidesUrl={event.data.slidesUrl}
199.                                     demosUrl={event.data.demosUrl}
200.                                 />
201.                             ))}
202.                         </div>
203.                     </div>
204.                 ) : (
205.                     <div class="empty-state">
206.                         <p>No events scheduled yet for this presentation.</p>
207.                     </div>
208.                 )}
209.             </div>
210. 
211.             <!-- Resources Tab -->
212.             <div class="tab-panel" data-tab="resources" style={currentTab !== 'resources' ? 'display: none;' : ''}>
213.                 {presentation.data.resources?.length > 0 ? (
214.                     <div class="resources-list">
215.                         <h2>Resources</h2>
216.                         <div class="resources-grid">
217.                             {presentation.data.resources.map((resource: any) => (
218.                                 <a href={resource.url} target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" class="resource-card">
219.                                     <span class="resource-icon">
220.                                         {resource.type === 'slides' && '📊'}
221.                                         {resource.type === 'video' && '🎬'}
222.                                         {resource.type === 'github' && '💻'}
223.                                         {resource.type === 'demo' && '🚀'}
224.                                         {resource.type === 'blog' && '📝'}
225.                                         {resource.type === 'download' && '⬇️'}
226.                                         {resource.type === 'other' && '🔗'}
227.                                     </span>
228.                                     <span class="resource-title">{resource.title}</span>
229.                                     {resource.description && <span class="resource-desc">{resource.description}</span>}
230.                                 </a>
231.                             ))}
232.                         </div>
233.                     </div>
234.                 ) : (
235.                     <div class="empty-state">
236.                         <p>Resources coming soon.</p>
237.                     </div>
238.                 )}
239.             </div>
240.         </div>
241.     </section>
242. 
243.     <!-- Book CTA -->
244.     <section class="book-cta">
245.         <div class="container">
246.             <div class="cta-content">
247.                 <p>
248.                     <strong>Want me to present this at your event?</strong><br>
249.                     Email <a href="mailto:chadgreen@chadgreen.com">chadgreen@chadgreen.com</a> to book this presentation.
250.                 </p>
251.                 <Button href="/contact?type=speaking" variant="primary">Contact Me</Button>
252.             </div>
253.         </div>
254.     </section>
255. 
256.     <TabSyncScript />
257. </BaseLayout>

477. 
478. <script>
479.     // Handle tab navigation without page reload
480.     const tabLinks = document.querySelectorAll('.tab-nav a');
481.     const tabPanels = document.querySelectorAll('.tab-panel');
482. 
483.     tabLinks.forEach(link => {
484.         link.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
485.             e.preventDefault();
486.             const url = new URL((link as HTMLAnchorElement).href);
487.             const tabId = url.searchParams.get('tab');
488. 
489.             // Update URL without reload
490.             window.history.pushState({}, '', url);
491. 
492.             // Update active tab
493.             tabLinks.forEach(l => l.classList.remove('tab-active'));
494.             link.classList.add('tab-active');
495. 
496.             // Show corresponding panel
497.             tabPanels.forEach(panel => {
498.                 if ((panel as HTMLElement).dataset.tab === tabId) {
499.                     (panel as HTMLElement).style.display = 'block';
500.                 } else {
501.                     (panel as HTMLElement).style.display = 'none';
502.                 }
503.             });
504.         });
505.     });
506. </script>
507.
```

```
2. import BaseLayout from '../../../layouts/BaseLayout.astro';
3. import { getCollection } from 'astro:content';
4. 
5. export async function getStaticPaths() {
6.     const presentations = await getCollection('presentations');
7. 
8.     return presentations.map((presentation) => ({
9.         params: { slug: presentation.id },
10.         props: {
11.             targetPath: `/presentations/${presentation.id}?tab=events`,
12.             presentationTitle: presentation.data.title,
13.         },
14.     }));
15. }
16. 
17. const { targetPath, presentationTitle } = Astro.props;
18. ---
19. 
20. <BaseLayout
21.     title={`${presentationTitle} Events`}
22.     description={`Redirecting to the events tab for ${presentationTitle}.`}
23.     canonicalUrl={new URL(targetPath, Astro.url.origin).toString()}
24. >
25.     <section class="section">
26.         <div class="container redirect-page">
27.             <h1>Redirecting...</h1>
28.             <p>If you are not redirected automatically, continue to <a id="redirect-link" href={targetPath}>events for this presentation</a>.</p>
29.         </div>
30.     </section>
31. 
32.     <script is:inline>
33.         const redirectLink = document.getElementById('redirect-link');
34.         if (redirectLink instanceof HTMLAnchorElement) {
35.             window.location.replace(redirectLink.href);
36.         }
37.     </script>
38. </BaseLayout>
```

```
1. ---
2. import { getCollection } from 'astro:content';
3. import EngagementPresentationPage from '@/components/presentations/EngagementPresentationPage.astro';
4. import { getCanonicalPath } from '@/lib/engagementPresentations';
5. 
6. export async function getStaticPaths() {
7.     const engagementPresentations = await getCollection('engagementPresentations');
8. 
9.     return engagementPresentations.map((engagement) => ({
10.         params: {
11.             slug: engagement.data.presentationSlug,
12.             eventSlug: engagement.data.eventSlug,
13.         },
14.         props: { engagement },
15.     }));
16. }
17. 
18. const { engagement } = Astro.props;
19. 
20. const [events, presentations] = await Promise.all([
21.     getCollection('events'),
22.     getCollection('presentations'),
23. ]);
24. 
25. const event = events.find((entry) => entry.id === engagement.data.eventSlug);
26. const presentation = presentations.find((entry) => entry.id === engagement.data.presentationSlug);
27. 
28. const canonicalPath = getCanonicalPath(engagement);
29. const canonicalUrl = new URL(canonicalPath, Astro.url.origin).toString();
30. ---
31. 
32. <EngagementPresentationPage
33.     engagement={engagement}
34.     event={event}
35.     presentation={presentation}
36.     canonicalUrl={canonicalUrl}
37. />
38.
```

</details>
