### **20. `src/components/presentations/EngagementPresentationPage.astro` - Engagement Presentation Component**
Reusable component for rendering engagement/session presentation pages with full content, details, and resources.

```astro
---
import BaseLayout from '../../layouts/BaseLayout.astro';
import Badge from '../ui/Badge.astro';
import Button from '../ui/Button.astro';
import { render, type CollectionEntry } from 'astro:content';
import { getResourceIcon } from '@/lib/engagementPresentations';

export interface Props {
    engagement: CollectionEntry\<'engagementPresentations'\>;
    event?: CollectionEntry\<'events'\>;
    presentation?: CollectionEntry\<'presentations'\>;
    canonicalUrl: string;
}

const { engagement, event, presentation, canonicalUrl } = Astro.props;
const { Content } = await render(engagement);

const displayTitle = engagement.data.sessionTitle || engagement.data.title;
const eventTitle = event?.data.title || engagement.data.eventSlug;
const presentationTitle = presentation?.data.title || engagement.data.presentationSlug;
const sessionImage = engagement.data.thumbnail || engagement.data.heroImage || presentation?.data.heroImage || '/images/presentations/default-thumbnail.svg';
const resourceLinks = engagement.data.links.length \> 0
    ? engagement.data.links
    : (presentation?.data.resources || []);
---

\<BaseLayout
    title={displayTitle}
    description={engagement.data.description}
    ogImage={engagement.data.heroImage || presentation?.data.heroImage}
    canonicalUrl={canonicalUrl}
\>
    \<section class="session-header"\>
        \<div class="container"\>
            \<div class="header-content"\>
                \<div class="header-image"\>
                    \<img
                        src={sessionImage}
                        alt={`${displayTitle} thumbnail`}
                        loading="eager"
                    /\>
                \</div\>
                \<div class="header-info"\>
                    \<div class="header-meta"\>
                        \<Badge variant="primary"\>Engagement Presentation\</Badge\>
                        \<Badge variant="secondary"\>{eventTitle}\</Badge\>
                    \</div\>

                    \<h1 class="session-title"\>{displayTitle}\</h1\>
                    \<p class="session-description"\>{engagement.data.description}\</p\>

                    \<div class="session-details"\>
                        {engagement.data.date && (
                            \<div class="detail-item"\>
                                \<span\>📅\</span\>
                                \<span\>{engagement.data.date.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'long', day: 'numeric', year: 'numeric', timeZone: 'UTC' })}\</span\>
                            \</div\>
                        )}
                        {(engagement.data.time || engagement.data.timeZone) && (
                            \<div class="detail-item"\>
                                \<span\>🕐\</span\>
                                \<span\>{engagement.data.time || 'TBA'}{engagement.data.timeZone ? ` ${engagement.data.timeZone}` : ''}\</span\>
                            \</div\>
                        )}
                        {engagement.data.room && (
                            \<div class="detail-item"\>
                                \<span\>📍\</span\>
                                \<span\>{engagement.data.room}\</span\>
                            \</div\>
                        )}
                    \</div\>

                    \<div class="header-actions"\>
                        \<Button href={`/speaking/${engagement.data.eventSlug}`} variant="outline"\>Event Details\</Button\>
                        \<Button href={`/presentations/${engagement.data.presentationSlug}`} variant="outline"\>Base Presentation\</Button\>
                        {engagement.data.sessionUrl && (
                            \<Button href={engagement.data.sessionUrl} variant="primary" external\>Session Listing\</Button\>
                        )}
                    \</div\>
                \</div\>
            \</div\>
        \</div\>
    \</section\>

    \<section class="section"\>
        \<div class="container"\>
            \<div class="content-grid"\>
                \<article class="session-content prose"\>
                    \<Content /\>
                \</article\>

                \<aside class="session-sidebar"\>
                    \<div class="sidebar-card"\>
                        \<h2\>Session Info\</h2\>
                        \<p\>\<strong\>Event:\</strong\> \<a href={`/speaking/${engagement.data.eventSlug}`}\>{eventTitle}\</a\>\</p\>
                        \<p\>\<strong\>Presentation:\</strong\> \<a href={`/presentations/${engagement.data.presentationSlug}`}\>{presentationTitle}\</a\>\</p\>
                    \</div\>
                \</aside\>
            \</div\>
        \</div\>
    \</section\>

    \<section class="section section-alt"\>
        \<div class="container"\>
            \<h2\>Session Resources\</h2\>
            {resourceLinks.length \> 0 ? (
                \<div class="resources-grid"\>
                    {resourceLinks.map((resource: any) =\> (
                        \<a href={resource.url} target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" class="resource-card"\>
                            \<span class="resource-icon"\>{getResourceIcon(resource.type)}\</span\>
                            \<span class="resource-title"\>{resource.title}\</span\>
                            {resource.description && \<span class="resource-description"\>{resource.description}\</span\>}
                        \</a\>
                    ))}
                \</div\>
            ) : (
                \<p class="resources-empty"\>Resources will be added soon.\</p\>
            )}
        \</div\>
    \</section\>
\</BaseLayout\>

\<style\>
    .session-header {
        background-color: var(--color-bg-secondary);
        padding: var(--spacing-10) 0;
    }

    .header-content {
        display: grid;
        grid-template-columns: 350px 1fr;
        gap: var(--spacing-8);
        align-items: start;
    }

    .header-image {
        border-radius: var(--radius-lg);
        overflow: hidden;
        box-shadow: var(--shadow-lg);
    }

    .header-image img {
        width: 100%;
        height: auto;
    }

    .header-meta {
        display: flex;
        flex-wrap: wrap;
        gap: var(--spacing-2);
        margin-bottom: var(--spacing-4);
    }

    .session-title {
        margin-bottom: var(--spacing-3);
        line-height: 1.2;
    }

    .session-description {
        font-size: var(--font-size-lg);
        color: var(--color-text-secondary);
        margin-bottom: var(--spacing-5);
    }

    .session-details {
        display: flex;
        flex-wrap: wrap;
        gap: var(--spacing-4);
        margin-bottom: var(--spacing-5);
    }

    .detail-item {
        display: inline-flex;
        align-items: center;
        gap: var(--spacing-2);
        color: var(--color-text-secondary);
        font-size: var(--font-size-sm);
    }

    .header-actions {
        display: flex;
        flex-wrap: wrap;
        gap: var(--spacing-2);
    }

    .content-grid {
        display: grid;
        grid-template-columns: 2fr 1fr;
        gap: var(--spacing-8);
    }

    .sidebar-card {
        background-color: var(--color-bg-secondary);
        border-radius: var(--radius-lg);
        padding: var(--spacing-5);
    }

    .sidebar-card h2 {
        margin-bottom: var(--spacing-3);
        font-size: var(--font-size-xl);
    }

    .sidebar-card p {
        margin-bottom: var(--spacing-2);
    }

    .section-alt {
        background-color: var(--color-bg-secondary);
    }

    .resources-grid {
        display: grid;
        grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(240px, 1fr));
        gap: var(--spacing-4);
        margin-top: var(--spacing-4);
    }

    .resource-card {
        display: flex;
        flex-direction: column;
        gap: var(--spacing-2);
        text-decoration: none;
        color: inherit;
        background-color: var(--color-bg-primary);
        border: 1px solid var(--color-border);
        border-radius: var(--radius-lg);
        padding: var(--spacing-4);
        transition: all var(--transition-fast);
    }

    .resource-card:hover {
        border-color: var(--color-primary);
        transform: translateY(-2px);
    }

    .resource-icon {
        font-size: var(--font-size-2xl);
    }

    .resource-title {
        font-weight: var(--font-weight-semibold);
    }

    .resource-description,
    .resources-empty {
        color: var(--color-text-secondary);
    }

    @media (max-width: 900px) {
        .header-content {
            grid-template-columns: 1fr;
        }

        .content-grid {
            grid-template-columns: 1fr;
        }
    }
\</style\>
```

---

## Summary

The chadgreen.com repository contains a comprehensive speaker and community leader portfolio with the following main page sections:

1. **Home** - Hero with recent events and blog posts
2. **Presentations** - Browsable presentation collection with filtering and individual presentation details
3. **Speaking Engagements** - Events listing (upcoming/past) with detailed event pages and sessions
4. **Meetups** - Community groups and meetup events
5. **Blog** - Articles with category and tag filtering
6. **About** - Complete biography with gallery and social links
7. **Contact** - Contact form and connection methods
8. **404** - Error page

Each page section supports filtering, tagging, and cross-linking. The engagement/session presentation pages bridge presentations with specific events, allowing event-specific scheduling details and customization.

51.                 <div class="presentations-grid grid grid-3" id="presentations-grid">
52.                     {presentations.map((presentation) => (
53.                         <div class="presentation-item" data-type={presentation.data.type}>
54.                             <PresentationCard
55.                                 title={presentation.data.title}
56.                                 description={presentation.data.description}
57.                                 slug={presentation.id}
58.                                 thumbnail={presentation.data.heroImage}

61.                                 featured={presentation.data.featured}

63.                         </div>
64.                     ))}
65.                 </div>
66.             ) : (
67.                 <div class="empty-state">
68.                     <h3>No Presentations Yet</h3>
69.                     <p>Check back soon for my collection of presentations.</p>
70.                 </div>
71.             )}
72.         </div>
73.     </section>
74. 
75.     <!-- Book CTA -->

78.             <div class="cta-content">

82.                 </p>
83.             </div>
84.         </div>
85.     </section>
86. </BaseLayout>

133.     }

159. <script>
160.     const filterBtns = document.querySelectorAll('.filter-btn');
161.     const items = document.querySelectorAll('.presentation-item');
162. 
163.     filterBtns.forEach(btn => {
164.         btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
165.             const filter = (btn as HTMLElement).dataset.filter;
166. 
167.             // Update active state
168.             filterBtns.forEach(b => b.classList.remove('active'));
169.             btn.classList.add('active');
170. 
171.             // Filter items
172.             items.forEach(item => {
173.                 const type = (item as HTMLElement).dataset.type;
174.                 if (filter === 'all' || type === filter) {
175.                     item.classList.remove('hidden');
176.                 } else {
177.                     item.classList.add('hidden');
178.                 }
179.             });
180.         });
181.     });
182. </script>
183.

2. import BaseLayout from '../../layouts/BaseLayout.astro';
3. import Badge from '../../components/ui/Badge.astro';
4. import Button from '../../components/ui/Button.astro';

6. import TabSyncScript from '../../components/ui/TabSyncScript.astro';
7. import EventCard from '../../components/cards/EventCard.astro';
8. import { getCollection, render } from 'astro:content';
9. import { marked } from 'marked';
10. 
11. export async function getStaticPaths() {
12.     const presentations = await getCollection('presentations');
13.     return presentations.map((presentation) => ({
14.         params: { slug: presentation.id },
15.         props: { presentation },
16.     }));
17. }
18. 
19. const { presentation } = Astro.props;
20. const { Content } = await render(presentation);
21. 
22. // Get related presentations
23. let relatedPresentations: any[] = [];
24. if (presentation.data.relatedPresentations?.length) {
25.     const allPresentations = await getCollection('presentations');
26.     relatedPresentations = allPresentations.filter(p => 
27.         presentation.data.relatedPresentations.includes(p.id)
28.     );
29. }
30. 
31. // Get events where this presentation was given
32. let presentationEvents: any[] = [];
33. try {
34.     const allEvents = await getCollection('events');
35.     presentationEvents = allEvents
36.         .filter(e => {
37.             if (!e.data.presentations) return false;
38.             // Handle both string format and object format
39.             return e.data.presentations.some((p: any) => {
40.                 const presentationId = typeof p === 'string' ? p : p.id;
41.                 return presentationId === presentation.id;
42.             });
43.         })
44.         .sort((a, b) => b.data.startDate.getTime() - a.data.startDate.getTime());
45. } catch (e) {
46.     // Collection might not exist yet
47. }
48. 
49. const tabs = [
50.     { id: 'abstract', label: 'Abstract' },
51.     { id: 'objectives', label: 'Learning Objectives' },
52.     { id: 'events', label: `Events (${presentationEvents.length})` },
53.     { id: 'resources', label: 'Resources' },
54. ];
55. 
56. // Get current tab from URL
57. const currentTab = Astro.url.searchParams.get('tab') || 'abstract';
58. 
59. const typeLabels: Record<string, string> = {
60.     'session': 'Session',
61.     'workshop': 'Workshop',
62.     'lightning-talk': 'Lightning Talk',
63.     'keynote': 'Keynote',
64.     'panel': 'Panel',
65.     'webinar': 'Webinar',
66. };
67. 
68. const statusLabels: Record<string, string> = {
69.     'active': 'Active',
70.     'retired': 'Retired',
71.     'in-development': 'In Development',
72. };
73. 
74. const statusVariants: Record<string, 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'outline' | 'success' | 'warning' | 'danger'> = {
75.     'active': 'success',
76.     'retired': 'outline',
77.     'in-development': 'warning',
78. };

478. <script>
479.     // Handle tab navigation without page reload
480.     const tabLinks = document.querySelectorAll('.tab-nav a');
481.     const tabPanels = document.querySelectorAll('.tab-panel');
482. 
483.     tabLinks.forEach(link => {
484.         link.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
485.             e.preventDefault();
486.             const url = new URL((link as HTMLAnchorElement).href);
487.             const tabId = url.searchParams.get('tab');
488. 
489.             // Update URL without reload
490.             window.history.pushState({}, '', url);
491. 
492.             // Update active tab
493.             tabLinks.forEach(l => l.classList.remove('tab-active'));
494.             link.classList.add('tab-active');
495. 
496.             // Show corresponding panel
497.             tabPanels.forEach(panel => {
498.                 if ((panel as HTMLElement).dataset.tab === tabId) {
499.                     (panel as HTMLElement).style.display = 'block';
500.                 } else {
501.                     (panel as HTMLElement).style.display = 'none';
502.                 }
503.             });
504.         });
505.     });
506. </script>
507.

### ✅ `view`

<details>
<summary>218 lines</summary>

```
1. ---
2. import BaseLayout from '../../layouts/BaseLayout.astro';
3. import PageHeader from '../../components/ui/PageHeader.astro';
4. import EventCard from '../../components/cards/EventCard.astro';
5. import { getCollection } from 'astro:content';
6. 
7. // Get all events
8. let allEvents: any[] = [];
9. try {
10.     allEvents = await getCollection('events');
11. } catch (e) {
12.     // Collection might not exist yet
13. }
14. 
15. // Separate upcoming and past events
16. const now = new Date();
17. const upcomingEvents = allEvents
18.     .filter(e => e.data.startDate >= now)
19.     .sort((a, b) => a.data.startDate.getTime() - b.data.startDate.getTime());
20. 
21. const pastEvents = allEvents
22.     .filter(e => e.data.startDate < now)
23.     .sort((a, b) => b.data.startDate.getTime() - a.data.startDate.getTime());
24. 
25. // Group past events by year
26. const pastEventsByYear = pastEvents.reduce((acc, event) => {
27.     const year = event.data.startDate.getFullYear();
28.     if (!acc[year]) acc[year] = [];
29.     acc[year].push(event);
30.     return acc;
31. }, {} as Record<number, any[]>);
32. 
33. const years = Object.keys(pastEventsByYear).sort((a, b) => Number(b) - Number(a));
34. 
35. function formatDateRange(start: Date, end?: Date): string {
36.     const utc = { timeZone: 'UTC' as const };
37.     if (!end || start.getTime() === end.getTime()) {
38.         return start.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'short', day: 'numeric', year: 'numeric', ...utc });
39.     }
40.     const sameMonth = start.getUTCMonth() === end.getUTCMonth() && start.getUTCFullYear() === end.getUTCFullYear();
41.     if (sameMonth) {
42.         return `${start.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'short', day: 'numeric', ...utc })} - ${end.getUTCDate()}, ${end.getUTCFullYear()}`;
43.     }
44.     return `${start.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'short', day: 'numeric', ...utc })} - ${end.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'short', day: 'numeric', year: 'numeric', ...utc })}`;
45. }
46. 
47. function formatLocation(loc: any): string {
48.     if (loc.virtual) return 'Virtual';
49.     const parts = [loc.city];
50.     if (loc.state) parts.push(loc.state);
51.     parts.push(loc.country);
52.     return parts.join(', ');
53. }
54. ---
55. 
56. <BaseLayout 
57.     title="Speaking Engagements" 
58.     description="Upcoming and past speaking engagements by Chad Green at conferences, meetups, and events worldwide."
59. >
60.     <PageHeader title="Speaking Engagements">
61.         <p>Conferences, meetups, and events where I've spoken or will be speaking</p>
62.     </PageHeader>
63. 
64.     <!-- Upcoming Events -->
65.     <section class="section">
66.         <div class="container">
67.             <h2 class="section-title">Upcoming Events</h2>
68. 
69.             {upcomingEvents.length > 0 ? (
70.                 <div class="upcoming-grid grid grid-3">
71.                     {upcomingEvents.map((event) => (
72.                         <EventCard
73.                             title={event.data.title}
74.                             slug={event.id}
75.                             startDate={event.data.startDate}
76.                             endDate={event.data.endDate}
77.                             location={event.data.location}
78.                             heroImage={event.data.heroImage}
79.                             eventType={event.data.eventType}
80.                             status="upcoming"
81.                             eventUrl={event.data.eventUrl}
82.                             featured={event.data.featured}
83.                         />
84.                     ))}
85.                 </div>
86.             ) : (
87.                 <div class="empty-state">
88.                     <p>No upcoming events scheduled. Check back soon!</p>
89.                 </div>
90.             )}
91.         </div>
92.     </section>
93. 
94.     <!-- Past Events by Year -->
95.     {years.length > 0 && (
96.         <section class="section section-alt">
97.             <div class="container">
98.                 <h2 class="section-title">Past Events</h2>
99. 
100.                 {years.map((year) => (
101.                     <div class="year-section">
102.                         <h3 class="year-heading">{year}</h3>
103.                         <div class="events-table">
104.                             <table>
105.                                 <thead>
106.                                     <tr>
107.                                         <th>Date</th>
108.                                         <th>Event</th>
109.                                         <th>Location</th>
110.                                     </tr>
111.                                 </thead>
112.                                 <tbody>
113.                                     {pastEventsByYear[Number(year)].map((event: any) => (
114.                                         <tr>
115.                                             <td class="date-col">
116.                                                 {formatDateRange(event.data.startDate, event.data.endDate)}
117.                                             </td>
118.                                             <td class="event-col">
119.                                                 <a href={`/speaking/${event.id}`}>{event.data.title}</a>
120.                                             </td>
121.                                             <td class="location-col">
122.                                                 {formatLocation(event.data.location)}
123.                                             </td>
124.                                         </tr>
125.                                     ))}
126.                                 </tbody>
127.                             </table>
128.                         </div>
129.                     </div>
130.                 ))}
131.             </div>
132.         </section>
133.     )}
134. </BaseLayout>
135.

28.

34.     
35.     linkedPresentations = event.data.presentations.map((p: any) => {

38.         const presentationData = allPresentations.find(pres => pres.id === presentationId);
39.         
40.         if (!presentationData) return null;
41.         
42.         // Add event-specific scheduling details
43.         const sessionSlug = sessionSlugByPresentationId.get(presentationId);
44. 
45.         return {
46.             ...presentationData,
47.             detailsHref: sessionSlug
48.                 ? `/speaking/${event.id}/presentations/${sessionSlug}`
49.                 : `/presentations/${presentationId}`,
50.             sessionName: typeof p === 'object' ? p.sessionName : undefined,
51.             scheduleDate: typeof p === 'object' ? p.date : undefined,

54.             scheduleRoom: typeof p === 'object' ? p.room : undefined,

56.         };
57.     }).filter(Boolean);
58. }

62.     if (!end || start.getTime() === end.getTime()) {

64.             weekday: 'long',
65.             month: 'long',

67.             year: 'numeric',

69.         });

74.     }

414. <script>
415.     // Client-side tab switching
416.     document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
417.         const tabLinks = document.querySelectorAll('.tab-item');
418.         const tabPanels = document.querySelectorAll('.tab-panel');
419.         
420.         tabLinks.forEach(link => {
421.             link.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
422.                 e.preventDefault();
423.                 const targetTab = link.getAttribute('data-tab');
424.                 
425.                 // Update active tab styling
426.                 tabLinks.forEach(l => l.classList.remove('tab-active'));
427.                 link.classList.add('tab-active');
428.                 
429.                 // Show/hide panels
430.                 tabPanels.forEach(panel => {
431.                     if (panel.getAttribute('data-tab') === targetTab) {
432.                         panel.style.display = '';
433.                     } else {
434.                         panel.style.display = 'none';
435.                     }
436.                 });
437.                 
438.                 // Update URL without reload
439.                 const url = new URL(window.location.href);
440.                 url.searchParams.set('tab', targetTab);
441.                 window.history.pushState({}, '', url);
442.             });
443.         });
444.     });
445. </script>
446.

2. import { getCollection } from 'astro:content';
3. import EngagementPresentationPage from '@/components/presentations/EngagementPresentationPage.astro';
4. import { getCanonicalPath } from '@/lib/engagementPresentations';
5. 
6. export async function getStaticPaths() {
7.     const engagementPresentations = await getCollection('engagementPresentations');
8. 
9.     return engagementPresentations.map((engagement) => ({
10.         params: {
11.             slug: engagement.data.presentationSlug,
12.             eventSlug: engagement.data.eventSlug,
13.         },
14.         props: { engagement },
15.     }));
16. }
17. 
18. const { engagement } = Astro.props;
19. 
20. const [events, presentations] = await Promise.all([
21.     getCollection('events'),
22.     getCollection('presentations'),
23. ]);

6. export async function getStaticPaths() {

15.     }));

26. const presentation = presentations.find((entry) => entry.id === engagement.data.presentationSlug);

9.     loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.md", base: "./src/content/presentations" }),

25. 
26.         // Learning objectives

28. 
29.         // Categorization
30.         tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
31. 
32.         // Related presentations
33.         relatedPresentations: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
34. 
35.         // Resources

39.             url: z.string(),
40.             description: z.string().optional(),
41.         })).optional(),
42. 
43.         // Media
44.         heroImage: z.string().optional(),
45. 
46.         // Status
47.         status: z.enum(['active', 'retired', 'in-development']).default('active'),
48.         featured: z.boolean().default(false),
49.     }),
50. });

<sub>⏱️ 470m 56s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Done. Here's how it works:

**Banner behavior:**
- Shown on: presentations listing, individual presentations, speaking/events listing, individual event pages, and engagement presentation pages
- **2026 events**: no banner (both the event page and its engagement pages are exempt)
- **Pre-2026 upcoming events** (e.g., ConFoo 2026): banner does show since they're in the 2026 year bucket

**To remove the banner as you validate:**
- **Individual presentation** → add `validated: true` to its frontmatter (also suppresses all linked engagement pages)
- **Individual event** → add `validated: true` to its frontmatter
- **Listing pages** → delete the `\<UnderConstructionBanner /\>` line from `presentations/index.astro` and `speaking/index.astro` when all content is ready

<sub>⏱️ 504m 55s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Now I understand the issue. The Azure Static Web Apps free tier has a max of 3 staging environments. PRs each create a staging environment that accumulates unless cleaned up. The fix: make PRs only validate the build (no staging deployment) while keeping production deploy on main push.

---

<sub>⏱️ 505m 29s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Done. Here's what changed:

**Root cause**: Every open PR creates a staging environment in Azure SWA. The free tier allows max 3. With multiple feature branches having open PRs, the limit was hit.

**Fix**: Split into two jobs:

| Job | Trigger | What it does |
|-----|---------|--------------|
| `build_validation_job` | PR open/sync/reopen | `npm ci` + `npm run build` — validates the build, **no staging env created** |
| `build_and_deploy_job` | Push to `main` or manual dispatch | Deploys to production via Azure SWA action |
| `close_pull_request_job` | PR closed | Cleans up any lingering staging environments |

PRs now get build validation without consuming a staging environment slot. Production only deploys from `main`. The close job remains as a safety net for any existing staging environments.

---

<sub>⏱️ 509m 31s</sub>

### 👤 User

But shouldn't we be publishing to a preview environment when creating a PR so that validations can be executed against the preview site? Maybe we clean up the staging environment when the PR is closed.

---

<sub>⏱️ 509m 58s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

You're right — the preview environment is valuable for validation. The original workflow already had the `close_pull_request_job` to clean up on PR close. The issue was likely stale environments from old PRs that weren't properly cleaned up. Let me revert to creating staging environments on PR, and verify the close job is solid.

-  # Validates PR builds without creating a staging environment, preventing
-  # "max staging environments" errors on the free Azure Static Web Apps tier.
-  build_validation_job:
-    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request' && github.event.action != 'closed'
-    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
-    name: Build Validation Job
-    steps:
-      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
-        with:
-          submodules: true
-
-      - name: Setup Node.js
-        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
-        with:
-          node-version: '20'
-          cache: 'npm'
-
-      - name: Cache Playwright browsers
-        uses: actions/cache@v4
-        with:
-          path: ~/.cache/ms-playwright
-          key: playwright-browsers-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
-          restore-keys: |
-            playwright-browsers-${{ runner.os }}-
-
-      - name: Install dependencies
-        run: npm ci
-
-      - name: Install Playwright browsers
-        run: npx playwright install --with-deps
-
-      - name: Build
-        run: npm run build
-
   build_and_deploy_job:
-    if: github.event_name == 'push' || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
+    if: github.event_name == 'push' || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch' || (github.event_name == 'pull_request' && github.event.action != 'closed')

     name: Build and Deploy Job

@@ -92,4 +58,5 @@ jobs:

The prior change was too aggressive.\n\nThe root cause of the 'max staging environments' error was stale\nenvironments from old PRs where the close job either didn't run or\nran before the close action was configured. Those are now resolved\nsince all open PRs have been merged.\n\nChanges:\n- Restore build_and_deploy_job to run on PR events (creates preview env)\n- Add repo_token to close_pull_request_job so it can post PR comments\n  and properly identify the environment to close\n\nGoing forward, every merged/closed PR will automatically clean up its\nstaging environment via close_pull_request_job.\n\nFixes #25\n\nCo-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>\" && git push",

<sub>⏱️ 510m 33s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Good call. Here's what the final state looks like:

**What was wrong**: The `close_pull_request_job` was missing `repo_token`, which is needed for the action to identify the correct PR/environment to close. Stale environments from past PRs (before this workflow was fully set up) accumulated until the limit was hit.

**The fix**:
- ✅ PRs still get staging/preview environments (for validation)
- ✅ Added `repo_token` to the close job so it can correctly identify and delete the staging environment when a PR is merged or closed
- The stale environments from before are gone (all open PRs have since been merged)

Going forward, every PR close/merge will automatically clean up its staging environment. Since this is a personal site, you're unlikely to have more than 3 open PRs simultaneously anyway.

---

<sub>⏱️ 571m 18s</sub>

### 👤 User

Draft a new blog article talking about my experience at this year's Microsoft Most Valuable Professional (MVP) Summit. https://summit.microsoft.com/en-us/. Because most, if not all, of the Summit is NDA material, I cannot publicly talk about what I learned, but I can talk about lots of AI talks (shocker). Should include something like I really enjoyed hearing about "redacted, and redacted, and also redacted." But then we can say, in all seriousness, that there were really good sessions on what Microsoft is working on, and the future looks bright. Talk about how my favorite session/period was a feedback session where we sat at a table with folks at Microsoft and talked about what we are doing, what we are hoping to do, the pains of getting things done, and how those things could be improved.

There needs to be information about how the best part is all the conversations outside the sessions with Microsoft folks and other MVPS. I purposely do not want to name-drop because that is tacky, but I did sit down with some really cool and important people this week.

Be sure to call out the cool must-dos like dinner at Seastar (https://seastarrestaurant.com/) - which should include a callout that Phil Japiske did get his seared tuna (those who know, know), Paddy Coyne’s (https://paddycoynes.net/) where many MVPs hang out for drinks after long days of sessions and gave us Catholics a good place to get fish and chips on Friday during lent, and the Party with Palermo (https://www.eventbrite.com/e/party-with-palermo-mvp-summit-featuring-net-rocks-registration-1979908843649?aff=oddtdtcreator) which this year featured .NET Rocks! (https://www.dotnetrocks.com/).

Big congrats need to go to Carl Franklin and Richard Campbell for recording their 2000th .NET Rocks! episode at the Party with Palermo.

There should be thanks for all of the Microsoft employees that put in so much effort to make the MVP Summit such a great event.

It is currently Friday, March 27; but date this blog article as Saturday, March 28 - which is when I finalize the article and actually post it. The article can start with how I'm on the plane on my way home from the MVP Summit.

### ✅ `glob`

---

<sub>⏱️ 571m 27s</sub>

### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\repos\chadgreen.com\src\content\blog\new-website-soft-launch.md.**

C:\repos\chadgreen.com\src\content\blog\new-website-soft-launch.md

<details>
<summary>180 lines</summary>

```
1. ---
2. title: "Soft Launch: The New chadgreen.com Is Live"
3. description: "After multiple restarts since 2022, the new chadgreen.com is finally live in soft-launch form. Here’s the full story of what changed, what’s working, and what’s next."
4. author: "Chad Green"
5. pubDate: 2026-02-21
6. heroImage: "/images/blog/new-website-soft-launch.webp"
7. heroImageAlt: "Soft Launch: The New chadgreen.com Is Live"
8. category: "Announcement"
9. tags: ["website", "announcement", "github-copilot", "speaking", "community"]
10. draft: false
11. featured: true
12. ---
13. 
14. I'm genuinely excited to announce the **soft relaunch** of [chadgreen.com](https://chadgreen.com). This is a long time coming, and I'm relieved to finally have something that better expresses the information I want to share.
15. 
16. The biggest highlights:
17. 
18. - The placeholder home page is gone (after over 3 years).
19. - Speaking and presentation pages are now part of a cohesive site experience.
20. - The site is usable today for upcoming events, sessions, and resources.
21. - The build time to MVP was dramatically accelerated using GitHub Copilot.
22. 
23. There is still missing content, and not every workflow is fully tested yet (the contact form is high on my list). But this launch is real progress and a much better way to share what I'm working on than a "coming soon" page.
24. 
25. ***
26. 
27. ## The Long Road Here (2022 to 2026)
28. 
29. If you have been around for a while, you may remember that I started rebuilding my website back in 2022.
30. 
31. At one point, my homepage literally pointed out that a new site was imminent:
32. 
33. ![Screenshot of the longtime chadgreen.com placeholder home page](/images/blog/chadgreen-homepage-placeholder.webp)
34. 
35. *The placeholder home page message that stayed up far longer than planned.*
36. 
37. The intent was real; the timeline was not.
38. 
39. Like many long-running personal projects, this one ran into reality: life happened, priorities shifted, and progress moved more slowly than expected.
40. 
41. I did manage to publish the speaking engagement and presentation pages (if you knew the URLs, you could access them). But after that initial push, I did not maintain those pages. The rest of the site remained in flux, and the homepage remained a placeholder signaling "in progress" for far too long.
42. 
43. ## Why This Took So Long
44. 
45. I had a clear concept for the site and what I wanted it to do, but I struggled to get the visual design to a level I felt good about.
46. 
47. I'm very comfortable architecting and designing robust backends. Front-end designs are a different muscle for me. I can absolutely build practical, business-ready UIs, but creating something that feels truly polished and distinctive takes more effort.
48. 
49. That gap between "works well" and "looks exactly right" is where this project kept slowing down.
50. 
51. ## The Temporary Repo That Became An Operational Site
52. 
53. While the primary site was in progress, I continued improving how I managed speaking content in GitHub.
54. 
55. For each presentation, I created:
56. 
57. - a **private repo** for development, working notes, and preparation, and
58. - a **public repo** for attendees that contained session details, slides, demos, and related resources.
59. 
60. To streamline the workflow, I built a GitHub Action for each private repo that automatically copies the relevant content to the public repo upon merge to main. This allows me to work privately, annotate my notes and development details, and then automatically push the polished session details (abstract, code samples, slides, etc.) to the public side. I built the original action during downtime at an MVP Summit, one of those productive moments that emerged from conference downtime.
61. 
62. In August 2022, I created my [Speaking Engagements repo](https://github.com/TaleLearnCode/SpeakingEngagements) as a consolidated public listing of where I was speaking. I merged the initial real data on September 3, 2022.
63. 
64. The repo included this note:
65. 
66. > This is a temporary listing of Chad Green's speaking engagements until chadgreen.com is rebuilt.
67. 
68. More than three years later, that "temporary" solution was still the public source of truth.
69. 
70. And honestly, it has been incredibly useful. Not only is it a place for people to see where I will be speaking, but I also rely on it every March when I compile my community contributions for [Microsoft MVP](https://mvp.microsoft.com/mvp) renewal.
71. 
72. ## Multiple Design Starts
73. 
74. My first experience with Figma was as an architect reviewing UI designs that a professional UX designer would build in Figma for my architectural solutions, and I would provide feedback via Figma comments. That was how I first learned how Figma works. Valuable experience, but I was in reviewer mode, not drive mode.
75. 
76. After the initial 2022 rebuild attempt stalled, I returned to Figma myself. I had to figure out how actually to build a design rather than just review one. I created a design that I was much happier with, a visual direction that felt closer to what I wanted for the site. I probably did not build the Figma correctly, and I highly doubt anyone could have taken that design and auto-converted it into working code. But it served its purpose: it was a clear vehicle for visually expressing what I was looking for, even if imperfectly executed.
77. 
78. Later, I worked with a close friend who is an exceptional front-end designer, architect, and developer. Together, we made strong progress on a version of the site that looked amazing. We were close to the finish line, but again, life changed schedules and momentum.
79. 
80. That work still mattered. It clarified the design language and the structure I wanted, even if we did not get to launch that exact implementation.
81. 
82. ***
83. 
84. ## What Changed One Friday Evening
85. 
86. On a recent Friday evening, I sat down and gave GitHub Copilot a highly detailed prompt. I provided:
87. 
88. - concrete product and content requirements,
89. - an exported image from the Figma direction,
90. - and iterative follow-up refinements.
91. 
92. That evening, I had a working site running locally.
93. 
94. From there, I kept iterating: tightening the structure, adjusting the pages, connecting the content, and shaping the current soft-launch state. The result is not perfect, but it is a functional MVP delivered dramatically faster than previous attempts.
95. 
96. ### Why Copilot Helped So Much
97. 
98. For this project, Copilot reduced the friction in exactly the areas where I was getting stuck:
99. 
100. - translating design intent into real implementation,
101. - scaffolding repetitive page and content patterns quickly,
102. - and accelerating iteration cycles so I could evaluate ideas in running code.
103. 
104. In other words, it helped me move from "I know what I want" to " I can ship this now."
105. 
106. ***
107. 
108. ## Current State of the Site
109. 
110. This launch is intentionally practical.
111. 
112. Right now, content for the upcoming 2026 speaking engagements and the associated presentations is available.
113. 
114. Known gaps still being worked:
115. 
116. - older/historical content backfill,
117. - expended long-form writing,
118. - and full validation of contact functionality.
119. 
120. So yes, this is a soft launch, but it is absolutely usable, and it gives me a foundation I can now improve in public.
121. 
122. ***
123. 
124. ## Technology Stack
125. 
126. The site is built on a modern, scalable foundation designed for both developer experience and performance (and, of course, it is serverless):
127. 
128. - **Astro:** A static site generator that excels at content-driven sites. Astro lets me write components in a variety of frameworks (or just HTML) and generates optimized static HTML by default. This keeps the site fast and simple to deploy.
129. - **Azure Static Web Apps:** Provides hosting for the static content directly from the `dist/` folder output by Astro. The free tier is perfect for personal projects, and I get a global CDN out of the box.
130. - **Azure Functions:** The API backend for dynamic workflows. An Azure Function endpoint handles contact form submissions, and the contact API integrates seamlessly with Azure Web Apps' `/api` routing.
131. - **TypeScript:** Used for type safety in components and scripts, which helped catch issues early while GitHub Copilot was scaffolding new pages.
132. - **Content Collections:** Astro's content collection system provides a structured, type-safe way to manage content (blog posts, presentations, events, meetups). This replaces a traditional database with organized markdown files in source control.
133. 
134. This tech stack keeps things simple, performant, and maintainable. Static generation means the site loads quickly, hosting is inexpensive, and content can live alongside code in GitHub. When I need server-side logic (like email from the contract form), Azure Functions provides that with minimal overhead.
135. 
136. ***
137. 
138. ## Site Design and Information Architecture
139. 
140. One of the goals of the design was not just visual polish, but also cleaner navigation between related content domains.
141. 
142. ![Site map and route relationship model for chadgreen.com](/images/blog/new-website-soft-launch-sitemap.svg)
143. 
144. ### Core Sections
145. 
146. - **Home (`/`)**: Entry point and high-level highlights.
147. - **Presentations (`/presentations`)**: Canonical presentation catalog.
148. - **Speaking Engagements (`/speaking`)**: Event-centric view of where sessions are delivered.
149. - **Meetups (`/meetups`)**: Meetup groups and individual meetup events.
150. - **Blog (`/blog`)**: Articles, updates, and long-form writing.
151. - **About (`/about`)**: Personal/professional background.
152. - **Contact (`/contact`)**: Reach-out workflow.
153. 
154. ### How the Sections Work Together
155. 
156. The site is designed so that presentation content and event content are connected without duplicating information:
157. 
158. - A presentation can be viewed in its own canonical context.
159. - The same presentation can also be discovered through speaking events.
160. - Meetup content has its own grouping (group → event), but remains consistent with the broader navigation model.
161. - Blog and informational pages provide context around the work, community, and updates.
162. 
163. This creates multiple discoverability paths for visitors: by topic/presentation, by event, by community activity, or by written content.
164. 
165. ***
166. 
167. ## What Happens Next
168. 
169. Now that the site is finally live, my focus is straightforward:
170. 
171. 1. Continue adding and backfilling content.
172. 2. Validate and harden code functionality (starting with contact flow).
173. 3. Refine design details over time rather than getting stuck on perfection.
174. 
175. This launch matters to me because it breaks the cycle of "almost done."
176. 
177. It also proves something practical: with strong requirements, iterative feedback, and the right tooling, you can move from stalled redesign to shipping a usable MVP much faster than expected. Yes, using AI agents sped things up, but getting to where I'm at now this fast was more about having those strong requirements and providing iterative feedback.
178. 
179. If you spot issues, missing links, or have suggestions, please [reach out](/contact). 
180.
```

</details>

### 💬 Copilot

Done! The draft is at `src/content/blog/mvp-summit-2026.md`. Here's a summary of what's in it:

- **Opens** on the plane heading home — immediately sets the tone
- **NDA section** with the tongue-in-cheek "[REDACTED], and [REDACTED], and also [REDACTED]" joke, followed by a genuine note that the sessions were impressive and the future looks bright
- **Feedback roundtable** called out as the favorite session — honest, no-slides conversation with Microsoft folks at the table
- **Hallway conversations** section — emphasizes the people without name-dropping
- **Must-dos**: Seastar (with the Phil Japiske / seared tuna callout), Paddy Coyne's (with the Lent/fish and chips angle), Party with Palermo featuring .NET Rocks!
- **Carl & Richard 2,000th episode** congratulations — given the weight the milestone deserves
- **Thank-you** to the Microsoft employees who make it happen
- **Closes** landing at the airport, energized

One thing you'll need: a hero image at `/public/images/blog/mvp-summit-2026.webp`. The post will build fine without it (it'll just show broken), so you can add one whenever you have a good photo from the week.

successfully downloaded text file (SHA: ecc4045946d0863e8f7d48d41f45b58d28795ca5)![Meet Your AI Team: Squad in Action](thumbnail.jpg)

## Short Abstract
Learn how to orchestrate teams of AI agents using GitHub Squad. See a live demo where autonomous agents collaborate to build a working application from zero.

## Abstract
GitHub Copilot is powerful, but what if you could scale from a solo AI assistant to an entire team of specialized agents working in parallel? This session introduces Squad: an open-source framework for multi-agent orchestration that lets you define teams of AI agents with specific roles, responsibilities, and expertise.

We'll progress from Copilot basics to the Copilot CLI, explore how Agents add autonomy, and see how Instructions and Skills let you customize agent behavior. Then, the climax: a live demo where a Squad team of 3 agents (Lead, Developer, Tester) stands up and builds a working application in real-time, showcasing true multi-agent collaboration.

Whether you're new to AI or exploring how to scale your use of Copilot, this session will show you what's possible when agents work as a team.

# Type
- 75-minute session

## Tags
- GitHub Copilot
- Copilot CLI
- Agents
- Multi-agent orchestration
- Squad
- AI automation
- AI teams
- Developer tools

## Learning Objectives
- Understand the capabilities of GitHub Copilot, Copilot CLI, and Agent Mode
- Learn how Instructions and Skills customize agent behavior  
- Discover how Squad orchestrates teams of autonomous AI agents
- See real-time collaboration between specialized agents (architecture, development, testing)
- Understand when to use solo Copilot vs. multi-agent Squad teams
- Leave with actionable next steps to experiment with AI agents in your own projects

## Presentations

| Event | Location | Date | Time | Room | Downloads |
|-------|:--------:|-----:|-----:|-----:|----------:|
| [Louisville .NET Meetup](https://www.meetup.com/louisville-dotnet/events/313892686) | Louisville, KY | 16-APR-2026 | 18:30 EDT | 700N Conference Room | Available Afterwards |
| [Cincy .NET User Group (CINNUG)](https://www.meetup.com/techlife-cincinnati/events/311751792) | Cincinnati, OH | 28-APR-2026 | 18:00 EDT | Office Key | Available Afterwards |

## Resources
There are no additional resources for this presentation.

Email [chadgreen@chadgreen.com](mailto:chadgreen@chadgreen.com?subject=Presentation%20Request:%20Presentation%20Title) to have Chad present this session at your event.
```

</details>

37. 
38. ## Generative AI for .NET Developers
39. 
40. ### Elevator Pitch
41. 
42. Learn to create intelligent .NET apps with Azure OpenAI! Discover how to leverage ChatGPT, understand AI concepts, and see a live demo of AI building AI apps.
43. 
44. ### Short Abstract
45. 
46. Discover how to use .NET, C#, and Azure to create amazing applications with OpenAI and other generative AI technologies. You will learn the fundamental concepts of this latest wave of AI, the benefits and challenges of using .NET with it, and the best practices and tips for developing intelligent apps. You will also see a live demo of how AI can build AI apps using .NET and OpenAI.
47. 
48. ### Full Abstract
49. 
50. Have you ever wondered how to create intelligent apps with .NET and Azure OpenAI? Do you want to learn how to leverage the power of large language models like ChatGPT to generate natural and engaging conversations? Do you want to see how AI can build AI apps? If you answered yes to these questions, this talk is for you!
51. 
52. In this talk, you will discover how to use .NET, C#, and Azure to create amazing applications with OpenAI and other generative AI technologies. You will learn the fundamental concepts of this latest wave of AI, the benefits and challenges of using .NET with it, and the best practices and tips for developing intelligent apps. You will also see a live demo of how AI can build AI apps using .NET and OpenAI.
53. 
54. ### Presentation Type
55. 
56. - 45-minute session
57. - 60-minute session
58. - 75-minute session
59. 
60. ### Target Audience
61. 
62. - .NET developers interested in AI and machine learning
63. - Developers looking to integrate generative AI into their applications
64. - Teams exploring Azure OpenAI capabilities
65. - Anyone curious about practical applications of ChatGPT and large language models
66.
```

</details>

<summary>26 lines</summary>

1. ---
2. title: "Messaging Patterns to Transform Your Cloud Architecture"
3. description: "Session details and resources for Louisville .NET Meetup April 2025: Messaging Patterns to Transform Your Cloud Architecture."
4. eventSlug: "louisville-dotnet-2025-04-18"
5. presentationSlug: "messaging-patterns-cloud-architecture"
6. sessionSlug: "messaging-patterns-cloud-architecture"

11. room: "Conference Room"
12. canonicalPath: "speaking-session"
13. ---
14. 
15. Thanks for attending **Messaging Patterns to Transform Your Cloud Architecture** at **Louisville .NET Meetup**!
16. 
17. ## Messaging Patterns to Transform Your Cloud Architecture
18. 
19. In today's fast-evolving cloud landscape, messaging patterns are the quiet enablers behind resilient, high-performance systems. This session unlocks the secrets of these paradigms—from Point-to-Point to the innovative Saga Pattern—to empower architects and developers to design systems that scale gracefully and respond dynamically. Through hands-on implementations and vivid real-world case studies, you'll gain technical mastery and a fresh perspective on integrating effective, future-proof messaging in your cloud solutions.
20. 
21. ### Learning Objectives
22. 
23. - Master Core Messaging Fundamentals: Understand the key principles behind messaging patterns such as Publish-Subscribe, Request-Reply, and more, and learn how they form the backbone of resilient cloud architectures.
24. - Implement Real-World Messaging Solutions: Follow detailed, step-by-step demonstrations and case studies that show how to deploy these patterns effectively in production environments.
25. - Transform and Elevate Your Cloud Strategy: Gain actionable best practices to enhance system scalability, reliability, and maintainability, arming you with innovative techniques to transform your cloud applications.
26.
```

</details>

<sub>⏱️ 1471m 19s</sub>

### 👤 User

For the Reflections on the 2026 Microsoft MVP Summit blog article, come up with social media posts pointing people to the article. I will post on X, LinkedIn, Bluesky, and Facebook - write meaningful posts for each.

---

<sub>⏱️ 1471m 52s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Here are platform-tailored posts for each:

---

### 🐦 X (Twitter)
*280 chars — punchy, hashtags, hook on the NDA joke*

\> Just landed from #MVPSummit. Can I share what I learned? Nope — NDA. 😅 But I *can* say the future looks bright, the feedback roundtable was 🔥, and Carl & Richard recorded their 2,000th @dotnetrocks episode live at the Party with Palermo. 🎙️
\>
\> My full reflections 👇
\> https://chadgreen.com/blog/mvp-summit-2026
\>
\> #MicrosoftMVP #dotnet #AI

---

### 💼 LinkedIn
*Professional but personal, no character limit, tell a story*

\> I just got home from the 2026 Microsoft MVP Summit, and I'm still processing everything.
\>
\> Most of what happens at Summit is under NDA — so I can't tell you what Microsoft is building. What I *can* tell you is that the sessions were genuinely impressive, the direction is clear, and the future looks very bright.
\>
\> My favorite session wasn't a keynote or a product demo. It was a feedback roundtable — a table full of Microsoft folks and MVPs just *talking*. No slides, no agenda theater. Just honest conversation about what we're building, what's getting in our way, and how things could be better. That's the kind of dialogue that makes the MVP program feel like a real partnership.
\>
\> And of course, the best parts happen outside the sessions: the hallway conversations, the dinners, the late nights at Paddy Coyne's. I sat down with some remarkable people this week, and those conversations were worth the trip on their own.
\>
\> Oh — and Carl Franklin and Richard Campbell recorded their **2,000th episode of .NET Rocks!** live at the Party with Palermo. What a milestone. 🎙️
\>
\> Full reflections on the week are on the blog — link in the comments.
\>
\> Thank you to every Microsoft employee who made Summit what it is. The effort shows.
\>
\> #MicrosoftMVP #MVPSummit #dotNET #AI #GitHub #Community

*(Drop the URL as the first comment to avoid LinkedIn suppressing reach)*

---

### 🦋 Bluesky
*Conversational, no hashtag culture, ~300 chars, slightly warmer tone than X*

\> Just landed from MVP Summit. The NDA means I can't tell you much — but I can say: lots of AI talks (shocker), an incredible feedback roundtable, and Carl & Richard just recorded their 2,000th .NET Rocks! episode live in front of us. What a week.
\>
\> Wrote it all up: https://chadgreen.com/blog/mvp-summit-2026

---

### 📘 Facebook
*Personal/casual, friendly tone, longer is fine, minimal hashtags*

\> Back on the ground after a week in Redmond for the Microsoft MVP Summit, and what a week it was.
\>
\> The sessions? Most of it is NDA — so I can't spill the details. But I genuinely walked away impressed. Microsoft is building good things, and the future is bright.
\>
\> My personal highlight was a feedback roundtable where we just sat at a table with Microsoft folks and had a real conversation — no slides, no scripted demos — just "here's what we're doing, here's what's hard, here's how you could help." More of that, always.
\>
\> Outside the sessions, the real magic happened: great conversations at Seastar (Phil did get his seared tuna 🐟), late nights at Paddy Coyne's, and the Party with Palermo — where Carl Franklin and Richard Campbell recorded the **2,000th episode of .NET Rocks!** LIVE. 2,000 episodes. That's extraordinary.
\>
\> Big thanks to all the Microsoft folks who pour so much into making Summit special. It shows every single year.
\>
\> Full write-up on the blog: https://chadgreen.com/blog/mvp-summit-2026

---

**A few notes:**
- The blog URL assumes `chadgreen.com/blog/mvp-summit-2026` — verify that slug once the PR is merged
- For LinkedIn, posting the URL as a first comment rather than in the post body generally gets better reach
- The Phil Japiske tuna reference is intentionally left in — it'll land with the right people 😄

<sub>⏱️ 9897m 32s</sub>

### ✅ `view`

**view the file at C:\repos\chadgreen.com\src\pages\speaking\[slug].astro.**

C:\repos\chadgreen.com\src\pages\speaking\[slug].astro

<details>
<summary>450 lines</summary>

```
1. ---
2. import BaseLayout from '../../layouts/BaseLayout.astro';
3. import Badge from '../../components/ui/Badge.astro';
4. import Button from '../../components/ui/Button.astro';
5. import TabNav from '../../components/ui/TabNav.astro';
6. import TabSyncScript from '../../components/ui/TabSyncScript.astro';
7. import PresentationCard from '../../components/cards/PresentationCard.astro';
8. import UnderConstructionBanner from '../../components/ui/UnderConstructionBanner.astro';
9. import { getCollection, render } from 'astro:content';
10. 
11. export async function getStaticPaths() {
12.     const events = await getCollection('events');
13.     return events.map((event) => ({
14.         params: { slug: event.id },
15.         props: { event },
16.     }));
17. }
18. 
19. const { event } = Astro.props;
20. const { Content } = await render(event);
21. 
22. // Get linked presentations with their event-specific details
23. let linkedPresentations: any[] = [];
24. if (event.data.presentations?.length) {
25.     const [allPresentations, allEngagementPresentations] = await Promise.all([
26.         getCollection('presentations'),
27.         getCollection('engagementPresentations'),
28.     ]);
29. 
30.     const sessionSlugByPresentationId = new Map(
31.         allEngagementPresentations
32.             .filter((entry) => entry.data.eventSlug === event.id)
33.             .map((entry) => [entry.data.presentationSlug, entry.data.sessionSlug])
34.     );
35.     
36.     linkedPresentations = event.data.presentations.map((p: any) => {
37.         // Handle both string format (old) and object format (new)
38.         const presentationId = typeof p === 'string' ? p : p.id;
39.         const presentationData = allPresentations.find(pres => pres.id === presentationId);
40.         
41.         if (!presentationData) return null;
42.         
43.         // Add event-specific scheduling details
44.         const sessionSlug = sessionSlugByPresentationId.get(presentationId);
45. 
46.         return {
47.             ...presentationData,
48.             detailsHref: sessionSlug
49.                 ? `/speaking/${event.id}/presentations/${sessionSlug}`
50.                 : `/presentations/${presentationId}`,
51.             sessionName: typeof p === 'object' ? p.sessionName : undefined,
52.             scheduleDate: typeof p === 'object' ? p.date : undefined,
53.             scheduleTime: typeof p === 'object' ? p.time : undefined,
54.             scheduleTimeZone: typeof p === 'object' ? p.timeZone : undefined,
55.             scheduleRoom: typeof p === 'object' ? p.room : undefined,
56.             sessionUrl: typeof p === 'object' ? p.sessionUrl : undefined,
57.         };
58.     }).filter(Boolean);
59. }
60. 
61. function formatDateRange(start: Date, end?: Date): string {
62.     const utc = { timeZone: 'UTC' as const };
63.     if (!end || start.getTime() === end.getTime()) {
64.         return start.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { 
65.             weekday: 'long',
66.             month: 'long', 
67.             day: 'numeric', 
68.             year: 'numeric',
69.             ...utc
70.         });
71.     }
72.     const sameMonth = start.getUTCMonth() === end.getUTCMonth() && start.getUTCFullYear() === end.getUTCFullYear();
73.     if (sameMonth) {
74.         return `${start.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'long', day: 'numeric', ...utc })} - ${end.getUTCDate()}, ${end.getUTCFullYear()}`;
75.     }
76.     return `${start.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'long', day: 'numeric', ...utc })} - ${end.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'long', day: 'numeric', year: 'numeric', ...utc })}`;
77. }
78. 
79. function formatLocation(loc: any): string {
80.     if (loc.virtual) return 'Virtual Event';
81.     const parts = [];
82.     if (loc.venue) parts.push(loc.venue);
83.     parts.push(loc.city);
84.     if (loc.state) parts.push(loc.state);
85.     parts.push(loc.country);
86.     return parts.join(', ');
87. }
88. 
89. // Determine if event is upcoming based on start date
90. const now = new Date();
91. const isUpcoming = event.data.startDate >= now;
92. const eventYear = event.data.startDate.getFullYear();
93. 
94. const tabs = [
95.     { id: 'about', label: 'About' },
96.     { id: 'presentations', label: `My Presentations (${linkedPresentations.length})` },
97. ];
98. 
99. // Get current tab from URL
100. const currentTab = Astro.url.searchParams.get('tab') || 'about';
101. 
102. const typeLabels: Record<string, string> = {
103.     'conference': 'Conference',
104.     'meetup': 'Meetup',
105.     'webinar': 'Webinar',
106.     'podcast': 'Podcast',
107.     'workshop': 'Workshop',
108.     'user-group': 'User Group',
109.     'corporate': 'Corporate Event',
110.     'other': 'Event',
111. };
112. ---
113. 
114. <BaseLayout 
115.     title={event.data.title}
116.     description={event.data.description || `Speaking engagement at ${event.data.title}`}
117.     ogImage={event.data.heroImage}
118. >
119.     <section class="event-header">
120.         <div class="container">
121.             <div class="header-content">
122.                 <div class="event-logo">
123.                     <img 
124.                         src={event.data.heroImage || '/images/events/default-event.svg'} 
125.                         alt={`${event.data.title} logo`}
126.                     />
127.                 </div>
128.                 <div class="event-info">
129.                     <div class="event-meta">
130.                         {isUpcoming ? (
131.                             <Badge variant="primary">Upcoming</Badge>
132.                         ) : (
133.                             <Badge variant="default">Past Event</Badge>
134.                         )}
135.                         <Badge variant="secondary">
136.                             {typeLabels[event.data.eventType] || event.data.eventType}
137.                         </Badge>
138.                         {event.data.location.virtual && <Badge variant="outline">Virtual</Badge>}
139.                     </div>
140.                     
141.                     <h1 class="event-title">{event.data.title}</h1>
142.                     
143.                     {event.data.description && (
144.                         <p class="event-subtitle">{event.data.description}</p>
145.                     )}
146.                     
147.                     <div class="event-details">
148.                         <div class="detail-item">
149.                             <span class="detail-icon">📅</span>
150.                             <span class="detail-text">{formatDateRange(event.data.startDate, event.data.endDate)}</span>
151.                         </div>
152.                         <div class="detail-item">
153.                             <span class="detail-icon">📍</span>
154.                             <span class="detail-text">{formatLocation(event.data.location)}</span>
155.                         </div>
156.                         {event.data.audience && (
157.                             <div class="detail-item">
158.                                 <span class="detail-icon">👥</span>
159.                                 <span class="detail-text">{event.data.audience} attendees</span>
160.                             </div>
161.                         )}
162.                     </div>
163.                 </div>
164.             </div>
165.         </div>
166.     </section>
167. 
168.     <UnderConstructionBanner show={eventYear !== 2026 && !event.data.validated} />
169. 
170.     <!-- Tab Navigation -->
171.     <div class="tabs-container">
172.         <div class="container">
173.             <TabNav 
174.                 tabs={tabs.map(t => ({ ...t, href: `?tab=${t.id}` }))} 
175.                 activeTab={currentTab} 
176.             />
177.         </div>
178.     </div>
179. 
180.     <!-- Tab Content -->
181.     <section class="section tab-content">
182.         <div class="container">
183.             <!-- About Tab -->
184.             <div class="tab-panel" data-tab="about" style={currentTab !== 'about' ? 'display: none;' : ''}>
185.                 <div class="prose">
186.                     <Content />
187.                 </div>
188.                 
189.                 {event.data.website && (
190.                     <div class="event-links">
191.                         <Button href={event.data.website} variant="primary">
192.                             Visit Event Website ↗
193.                         </Button>
194.                     </div>
195.                 )}
196. 
197.                 {event.data.photos && event.data.photos.length > 0 && (
198.                     <div class="photos-section">
199.                         <h2>Photos</h2>
200.                         <div class="photos-grid">
201.                             {event.data.photos.map((photo: string) => (
202.                                 <div class="photo-item">
203.                                     <img src={photo} alt="Event photo" loading="lazy" />
204.                                 </div>
205.                             ))}
206.                         </div>
207.                     </div>
208.                 )}
209.             </div>
210. 
211.             <!-- Presentations Tab -->
212.             <div class="tab-panel" data-tab="presentations" style={currentTab !== 'presentations' ? 'display: none;' : ''}>
213.                 {linkedPresentations.length > 0 ? (
214.                     <div class="presentations-list">
215.                         <h2>My Sessions at This Event</h2>
216.                         <div class="grid grid-3">
217.                             {linkedPresentations.map((presentation) => (
218.                                 <PresentationCard
219.                                     title={presentation.data.title}
220.                                     description={presentation.data.description}
221.                                     slug={presentation.id}
222.                                     detailsHref={presentation.detailsHref}
223.                                     thumbnail={presentation.data.heroImage}
224.                                     presentationType={presentation.data.type}
225.                                     tags={presentation.data.tags}
226.                                     showSchedule={true}
227.                                     sessionName={presentation.sessionName}
228.                                     scheduleDate={presentation.scheduleDate}
229.                                     scheduleTime={presentation.scheduleTime}
230.                                     scheduleTimeZone={presentation.scheduleTimeZone}
231.                                     scheduleRoom={presentation.scheduleRoom}
232.                                     sessionUrl={presentation.sessionUrl}
233.                                 />
234.                             ))}
235.                         </div>
236.                     </div>
237.                 ) : (
238.                     <div class="empty-state">
239.                         <p>No presentations listed for this event yet.</p>
240.                     </div>
241.                 )}
242.             </div>
243.         </div>
244.     </section>
245. 
246.     <TabSyncScript />
247. </BaseLayout>
248. 
249. <style>
250.     .event-header {
251.         background-color: var(--color-bg-secondary);
252.         padding: var(--spacing-10) 0;
253.     }
254. 
255.     .header-content {
256.         display: grid;
257.         grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr;
258.         gap: var(--spacing-8);
259.         align-items: start;

273.     }
274.

276.         display: flex;

278.         gap: var(--spacing-2);

280.     }
281.

294.

296.         margin-bottom: var(--spacing-4);
297.     }
298.

318.         gap: var(--spacing-3);
319.     }
320.

322.         background-color: var(--color-bg-primary);
323.         border-bottom: 1px solid var(--color-border);
324.         position: sticky;
325.         top: 0;
326.         z-index: 10;
327.     }
328. 
329.     .tab-content {
330.         padding: var(--spacing-10) 0;
331.     }
332. 
333.     .tab-panel {
334.         animation: fadeIn 0.3s ease-in;
335.     }
336.

338.         from {
339.             opacity: 0;
340.             transform: translateY(10px);
341.         }
342.         to {
343.             opacity: 1;
344.             transform: translateY(0);
345.         }
346.     }
347. 
348.     .prose {
349.         max-width: 800px;
350.         line-height: 1.7;
351.         margin-bottom: var(--spacing-8);
352.     }
353.

355.         margin-top: var(--spacing-8);
356.         margin-bottom: var(--spacing-4);
357.     }
358.

361.     }

364.         margin-top: var(--spacing-6);
365.     }
366.

368.         margin-bottom: var(--spacing-6);
369.     }

374.         color: var(--color-text-secondary);
375.     }

379.     }

382.         margin-bottom: var(--spacing-6);
383.     }
384.

386.         display: grid;
387.         grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(250px, 1fr));
388.         gap: var(--spacing-4);
389.     }
390.

400.         object-fit: cover;

405.             grid-template-columns: 1fr;
406.         }
407. 
408.         .event-logo {

410.         }

412.         .event-title {
413.             font-size: var(--font-size-3xl);
414.         }
415.     }
416. </style>
417. 
418. <script>
419.     // Client-side tab switching
420.     document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
421.         const tabLinks = document.querySelectorAll('.tab-item');
422.         const tabPanels = document.querySelectorAll('.tab-panel');
423.         
424.         tabLinks.forEach(link => {
425.             link.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
426.                 e.preventDefault();
427.                 const targetTab = link.getAttribute('data-tab');
428.                 
429.                 // Update active tab styling
430.                 tabLinks.forEach(l => l.classList.remove('tab-active'));
431.                 link.classList.add('tab-active');
432.                 
433.                 // Show/hide panels
434.                 tabPanels.forEach(panel => {
435.                     if (panel.getAttribute('data-tab') === targetTab) {
436.                         panel.style.display = '';
437.                     } else {
438.                         panel.style.display = 'none';
439.                     }
440.                 });
441.                 
442.                 // Update URL without reload
443.                 const url = new URL(window.location.href);
444.                 url.searchParams.set('tab', targetTab);
445.                 window.history.pushState({}, '', url);
446.             });
447.         });
448.     });
449. </script>
450.
```

<sub>⏱️ 9897m 38s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

I found the issue! Look at lines 36-58 of the event page. The code is trying to extract scheduling info from the event's `presentations` array (lines 51-56), but in the event file, the presentations are defined WITHOUT scheduling details:

```yaml
presentations:
  - id: "messaging-patterns-cloud-architecture"
  - id: "navigating-maze-architecture-decisions"
```

They need to include the scheduling info like this:

```yaml
presentations:
  - id: "messaging-patterns-cloud-architecture"
    date: "April 9, 2026"
    time: "9:00 AM"
    timeZone: "EDT"
    room: "Room XYZ"
  - id: "navigating-maze-architecture-decisions"
    date: "April 10, 2026"
    time: "2:00 PM"
    timeZone: "EDT"
    room: "Room ABC"
```

Do you have the actual scheduled times for these two sessions at CodeStock 2026? Once you provide those, I can update the event file to display them correctly on the listings page.

<sub>⏱️ 10031m 5s</sub>

### 👤 User

I have added the presentations/architecting-with-ai.md document. How do I remove the "Work in Progress" banner from the rendered page?

---

<sub>⏱️ 10031m 13s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Add `validated: true` to the frontmatter of your presentation file.

In `src/content/presentations/architecting-with-ai.md`, add this line to the YAML frontmatter:

```yaml
---
title: "Architecting with AI"
description: "..."
...
validated: true
---
```

The banner only shows when `validated` is `false` (or missing). Once you set it to `true`, the banner disappears from both the individual presentation page and any linked engagement/session pages.

You can do this selectively for pages you've confirmed are accurate. Listing pages (`/presentations` and `/speaking`) always show the banner until you remove the `\<UnderConstructionBanner /\>` component from those templates.

---

<sub>⏱️ 10035m 29s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Done! The issue was that the event dates were set to **August 14-15, 2025** instead of **April 9-10, 2026**. Since those 2025 dates are in the past, the event was filtered into the past events section and likely not showing due to the `featured: false` flag.

The Speaking Engagements page should now display Beer City Code 2026 in the upcoming events list. Try running `npm run dev` to see the updated page.

<sub>⏱️ 10093m 24s</sub>

### 👤 User

What are the possible presentation statuses I can use?

---

<sub>⏱️ 10093m 33s</sub>

### 💬 Copilot

Based on the codebase conventions, the valid **presentation status** values are:

1. **`active`** — Actively used presentation (currently offered)
2. **`retired`** — No longer delivered (archived)
3. **`in-development`** — Work in progress (not yet ready)

These are defined in the content schema (`src/content.config.ts`). Use them in your presentation frontmatter's `status` field to control visibility and filtering across the site.

---

170.             btn.classList.add('active');

175.                 if (filter === 'all' || type === filter) {

177.                 } else {

179.                 }

182.     });
183. </script>
